399	\paragraph{End-to-end map: Sub-builds 1--3 \(\Rightarrow\) closure of
400	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) (\ref{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}, non-zero branch L535).}
401	The L535 closure target is: for an integral, locally-Noetherian scheme
402	\(X\) satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) and a non-zero
403	\(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\),
404	\((\texttt{Function.support}\,(Y \mapsto \texttt{order}\,Y\,f)).\texttt{Finite}\).
405	Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV proves this by reducing to an
406	affine open neighbourhood and citing height-1 prime decomposition
407	on a Noetherian ring. The project decomposes the proof into three
408	project-local Sub-builds plus a terminal closure step:
409	
410	\begin{itemize}
411	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 1 (CLOSED iter-200, 8 axiom-clean decls
412	    L153--L305).} Open-immersion descent of the
413	    \(\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) bundle: bijection between
414	    prime divisors of \(X\) lying in an open \(U\) and prime divisors
415	    of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\); stalk-isomorphism wrapper around
416	    Mathlib's \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\); descent of the
417	    \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) class.
418	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 2 (CLOSED iter-201, 6 axiom-clean decls
419	    L247--L519).} Naturality of \(\texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\) across a
420	    ring isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) of Noetherian-domain stalks of
421	    Krull dim \(\leq 1\), with compatible fraction-field iso
422	    \(K_R \simeq K_S\). The realised modular decomposition is
423	    4 private \(\texttt{Ring.*}\) helpers
424	    (\(\texttt{ord\_ringEquiv}\),
425	     \(\texttt{nonZeroDivisors\_ringEquiv}\),
426	     \(\texttt{ordMonoidWithZeroHom\_ringEquiv}\),
427	     \(\texttt{ordFrac\_ringEquiv}\) anchor), plus the public
428	    function-field iso \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso}\)
429	    (\ref{def:functionFieldIso}) and the parameterised packaging
430	    \(\texttt{Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac\_stalkIso\_naturality}\)
431	    (private). The \(\texttt{ordFrac\_ringEquiv}\) anchor proof
432	    case-splits on \(x = 0\); in the non-zero branch it obtains
433	    \((a, b \in \mathrm{nzd}\,R)\) from
434	    \(\texttt{IsLocalization.surj}\), lifts the numerator via
435	    \(\texttt{mem\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_ne\_zero}\), expresses both
436	    sides as \(\texttt{mk'}\,K_R\,a\,\langle b, \cdot\rangle\) /
437	    \(\texttt{mk'}\,K_S\,(e\,a)\,\langle e\,b, \cdot\rangle\), applies
438	    \(\texttt{Ring.ordFrac\_eq\_div}\), and closes via
439	    \(\texttt{ordMonoidWithZeroHom\_ringEquiv}\) on numerator and
440	    denominator.
441	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 3 (iter-202 target, \(\sim 30\)--\(60\) LOC).}
442	    Discharge the \(\texttt{h\_compat}\) hypothesis of
443	    \(\texttt{Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac\_stalkIso\_naturality}\)
444	    by specialising \(e_K\) to
445	    \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso}\,U\) (\ref{def:functionFieldIso}).
446	    The compatibility reduces to a naturality of
447	    \(\texttt{stalkSpecializes}\) (to the generic point) along the
448	    open-immersion stalk iso \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\); the
449	    morphism-level form is the equation
450	    \(\texttt{stalkIso} \,\ggg\, \texttt{stalkSpecializes}\,(\text{gp}_X \rightsquigarrow Y)
451	     = \texttt{stalkSpecializes}\,(\text{gp}_U \rightsquigarrow Y')
452	       \,\ggg\, \texttt{functionFieldIso}\)
453	    in \(\texttt{CommRingCat}\). Reference: Mathlib's
454	    \(\texttt{PresheafedSpace.stalkMap.stalkSpecializes\_stalkMap\_assoc}\)
455	    (\texttt{Geometry.RingedSpace.Stalks}). Naturality of
456	    \(\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}\) across the stalk iso of
457	    Sub-build 1 follows by the resulting equation: for
458	    \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\)
459	    and \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\),
460	    \(\texttt{order}\,Y\,f
461	     = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,U\,Y\,h)\,f'\)
462	    with \(f'\) the image of \(f\) under
463	    \(X.\texttt{functionField} \simeq U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{functionField}\).
464	    Direct consumer of Sub-build 2.
465	  \item \textbf{Terminal closure (iter-203 target, \(\sim 40\)--\(80\) LOC).}
466	    Given a non-zero \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\):
467	    (a) reduce to a finite affine cover \(X = \bigcup_{i = 1}^N U_i\)
468	    (Hartshorne II.6 hypothesis; project-side
469	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]} or just
470	    \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]}); (b) by Sub-build 3, every
471	    \(Y \in \texttt{support}\) lies in some \(U_i\) and has
472	    \(\texttt{order}\,Y\,f = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,
473	     U_i\,Y\,h)\,f_i\) for \(f_i\) the corresponding fraction-field
474	    element on \(U_i\); (c) each \(U_i\) is affine Noetherian, so the
475	    height-1 prime decomposition of \(\mathrm{div}(f_i)\) in
476	    \(\Gamma(U_i, \mathcal O)\) is finite by Mathlib's
477	    \(\texttt{Ideal.finite\_minimalPrimes\_of\_isNoetherianRing}\)
478	    applied to the principal ideal \((f_i)\); (d) bound
479	    \(|\texttt{support}|\) by the sum of the finitely many
480	    \(|\texttt{support of div}(f_i)|\) terms; (e) conclude finiteness.
481	\end{itemize}
482	
483	The chain terminates: each Sub-build is bounded in scope to a
484	specific project-local naturality / descent step; Sub-build 3 is a
485	direct consumer of Sub-build 2 with no further branching; the
486	terminal closure is bounded by the cardinality of a finite affine
487	cover. The structural blocker around the
488	\(\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}\) vs.\
489	\(\texttt{[IsNoetherian X]}\) hypothesis gap (consumer-propagation
490	USER-blocked, see \texttt{STRATEGY.md} A.4.a row) does \emph{not}
491	affect Sub-build 2 or Sub-build 3, which are stated on the open
492	chart \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) and consume only local hypotheses; the
493	terminal closure step is where the hypothesis-strength choice
494	becomes load-bearing.
495	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
496	\begin{definition}
497	\leanok
498	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
499	  \label{def:order_at_point}
500	  
501	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
502	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
503	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
504	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
505	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
506	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
507	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
508	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
509	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
510	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
511	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
512	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
513	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
514	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
515	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
516	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
517	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
518	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
519	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
520	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
521	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
522	  \[
523	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
524	  \]
525	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
526	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
527	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
528	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
529	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
530	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
531	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
532	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
533	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
534	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
535	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
536	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
537	  \begin{quote}
538	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
539	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
540	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
541	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
542	  \end{quote}
543	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
544	  see below). The body is
545	  \[
546	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
547	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
548	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
549	      \bigr).
550	  \]
551	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
552	  \begin{itemize}
553	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
554	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
555	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
556	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
557	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
558	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
559	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
560	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
561	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
562	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
563	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
564	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
565	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
566	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
567	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
568	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
569	      X.functionField} via
570	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
571	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
572	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
573	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
574	      and from the explicitly threaded
575	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
576	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
577	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
578	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
579	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
580	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
581	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
582	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
583	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
584	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
585	  \end{itemize}
586	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
587	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
588	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
589	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
590	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
591	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
592	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
593	  signature.
594	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
595	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
596	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
597	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
598	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
599	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
600	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
601	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
602	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
603	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
604	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
605	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
606	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
607	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
608	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
609	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
610	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
611	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
612	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
613	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
614	\end{definition}
615	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
616	\begin{definition}
617	\leanok
618	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
619	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
620	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
621	  
622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
623	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
624	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
625	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
626	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
627	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
628	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
629	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
630	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
631	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
632	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
633	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
634	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
635	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
636	  \[
637	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
638	  \]
639	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
640	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
641	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
642	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
643	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
644	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
645	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
646	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
647	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
648	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
649	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
650	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
651	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
652	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
653	  codim-\(1\) regime:
654	  \[
655	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
656	      \begin{cases}
657	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
658	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
659	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
660	      \end{cases}
661	  \]
662	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
663	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
664	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
665	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
666	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
667	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
668	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
669	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
670	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
671	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
672	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
673	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
674	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
675	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
676	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
677	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
678	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
679	\end{definition}
680	\begin{lemma}
681	\leanok
682	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
683	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
684	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
685	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
686	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
687	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
688	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
689	  then
690	  \[
691	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
692	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
693	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
694	  \]
695	\end{lemma}
696	\begin{proof}
697	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
698	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
699	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
700	\end{proof}
701	\begin{lemma}
702	\leanok
703	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
704	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
706	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
707	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
708	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
709	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
710	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
711	  scheme), then
712	  \[
713	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
714	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
715	  \]
716	\end{lemma}
717	\begin{proof}
718	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
719	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
720	  negative branch using the hypothesis
721	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
722	\end{proof}
723	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
724	\begin{definition}
725	\leanok
726	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
727	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
728	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
729	  
730	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
731	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
732	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
733	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
734	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
735	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
736	  nonsingular curve).}
737	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
738	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
739	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
740	  \[
741	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
742	  \]
743	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
744	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
745	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
746	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
747	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
748	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
749	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
750	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
751	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
752	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
753	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
754	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
755	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
756	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
757	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
758	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
759	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
760	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
761	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
762	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
763	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
764	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
765	\end{definition}
766	\begin{theorem}
767	\leanok
768	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
769	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
770	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
771	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
772	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
773	  homomorphism
774	  \[
775	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
776	    \qquad
777	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
778	    \qquad
779	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
780	  \]
781	\end{theorem}
782	\begin{proof}
783	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
784	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
785	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
786	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
787	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
788	\end{proof}
789	\section{Principal divisors}
790	\begin{lemma}
791	
792	\leanok
793	[Finite support of the order function — Hartshorne 6.1 / Stacks 02RV]
794	  \label{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
795	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_order_finite_support}
796	  % \lean{...} corrected iter-199 review: the iter-199 plan-phase
797	  % pin used the wrong namespace (".Scheme.") and the Lean theorem at
798	  % WeilDivisor.lean:357 sits in `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` only
799	  % (Scheme.RationalMap closed at L159). The theorem is also declared
800	  % `private` — sync_leanok may not resolve private decls cleanly. The
801	  % chapter pin is retained as documentation for the future-closed
802	  % version; the iter-199 plan agent's directive to consume this via
803	  % the `[IsNoetherian X]` strengthening remains the closure path.
804	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:prime_divisor}
805	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (read from
806	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
807	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
808	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
809	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
810	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (a.k.a.\ Stacks 02RV).}
811	
812	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral scheme that is regular in
813	  codimension one, and let \(f \in K(X)\) be a nonzero rational function.
814	  Then \(\{Y \in X^{(1)} \mid \ord_Y(f) \ne 0\}\) is finite.
815	\end{lemma}
816	
817	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open
818	% affine subset of \(X\) on which \(f\) is regular, \(f \in A\). Then for any
819	% prime divisor \(Y\) which meets \(U\), \(Y \cap U\) corresponds to a minimal
820	% prime ideal \(\mathfrak p\) of \(A\) containing \(f\). But these are finite
821	% in number (II, 3.10). On the other hand, \(X - U\) is a proper closed
822	% subset of \(X\), so it can contain only finitely many prime divisors of
823	% \(X\) [\dots]"
824	\begin{proof}
825	  \uses{def:order_at_point}
826	  Following Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV:
827	  \begin{enumerate}
828	  \item Pick a nonempty affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R \subset X\) on
829	    which \(f\) is regular. Then \(R\) is a Noetherian integral domain.
830	  \item For each prime divisor \(Y\) meeting \(U\), the corresponding
831	    prime \(\mathfrak p_Y\) is a height-1 prime of \(R\); the height-1 primes
832	    containing \(f \cdot 1\) are precisely the minimal primes of
833	    \((f)\), which by Krull's Hauptidealsatz form a finite set (Stacks tag
834	    00KZ / Atiyah--Macdonald 11.16). Of those, \(\ord_Y(f) \ne 0\) holds
835	    only at the finitely many minimal primes of \((f)\) over \(R\).
836	  \item The remaining prime divisors \(Y\) lie in the closed complement
837	    \(X \setminus U\), which is a proper closed subset of \(X\); the
838	    Noetherian topological structure of \(X\) (Stacks 04ZE) bounds the
839	    irreducible components of \(X \setminus U\) of codimension one in
840	    \(X\) by a finite number. For each such component, the stalk
841	    \(\mathcal O_{X, Y}\) is a DVR (regularity in codim 1) and \(f\) has
842	    finite valuation; together these contribute only finitely many
843	    nonzero values to the order function.
844	  \item Combining (2) and (3) gives the finiteness of the support.
845	  \end{enumerate}
846	  \emph{Hypothesis health.} The argument in (3) uses that \(X\) is
847	  Noetherian as a topological space (so \(X \setminus U\) has finitely
848	  many irreducible components). For \([X]\) only locally Noetherian +
849	  integral (without quasi-compactness), one can produce
850	  counter-examples with infinitely many disjoint codim-1 prime
851	  divisors outside \(U\). The Lean signature thus takes
852	  \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]} (or equivalently
853	  \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]}). The hybrid
854	  WeilDivisor file pins the public-facing declaration under the
855	  weaker \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]} hypothesis and exposes the
856	  \texttt{[CompactSpace X]} (or full Noetherian) strengthening
857	  as the substantive sub-claim that this lemma resolves.
858	\end{proof}

861	\leanok
862	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point, lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
863	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
864	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
865	  
866	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
867	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
868	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
869	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
870	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
871	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
872	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
873	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
874	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
875	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
876	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
877	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
878	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
879	  p.~131.}
880	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
881	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
882	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
883	  \[
884	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
885	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
886	  \]
887	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
888	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
889	  the same definition specialises to
890	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
891	  sum over closed points.
892	\end{definition}
893	\begin{theorem}
894	\leanok
895	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
896	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
897	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
898	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
899	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
900	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
901	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
902	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
903	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
904	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
905	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
906	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
907	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
908	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
909	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
910	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
911	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
912	  \[
913	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
914	    \qquad
915	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
916	    \qquad
917	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
918	  \]
919	\end{theorem}
920	\begin{proof}
921	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
922	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
923	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
924	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
925	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
926	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
927	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
928	  identities.
929	\end{proof}
930	\begin{theorem}
931	\leanok
932	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
933	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
934	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
935	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
936	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
937	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
938	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
939	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
940	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
941	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
942	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
943	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
944	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
945	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
946	  \[
947	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
948	  \]
949	\end{theorem}
950	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
951	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
952	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
953	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
954	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
955	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
956	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
957	\begin{proof}
958	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
959	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
960	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
961	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
962	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
963	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
964	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
965	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
966	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
967	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
968	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
969	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
970	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
971	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
972	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
973	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
974	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
975	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
976	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
977	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
978	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
979	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
980	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
981	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
982	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for

11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the

13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}

15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit

21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed

23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
150	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
151	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
152	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
153	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
154	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
155	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
156	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
157	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
158	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
159	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
160	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
161	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
162	\end{proof}

164	\section{Projective dimension}
165	\label{sec:ab_pd}
166	
167	\begin{definition}
168	
169	\leanok
170	  [Projective dimension]
171	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
172	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
173	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
174	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
175	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
176	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
177	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
178	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
179	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
180	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
181	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
182	  \[
183	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
184	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
185	  \]
186	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
187	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
188	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
189	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
190	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
191	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
192	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
193	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
194	\end{definition}
195	
196	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
197	\begin{itemize}
198	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
199	    iff \(M\) is free);
200	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
201	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
202	\end{itemize}
203	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
204	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
205	
206	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
207	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
208	
209	\begin{lemma}
210	
211	\leanok
212	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
213	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
214	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
215	  
216	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
217	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
220	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
221	  %  \begin{enumerate}
222	  %  \item
223	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
228	  %  \end{enumerate}"
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
230	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
231	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
232	  \(R\)-modules. Then
233	  \begin{enumerate}
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
237	  \end{enumerate}
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
241	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
242	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
243	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
244	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
245	%  from Lemma REF."
246	\begin{proof}
247	  
248	  
249	  
250	  
251	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
252	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
253	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
254	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
255	  \[
256	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
259	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
260	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
261	  \]
262	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
263	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
264	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
265	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
266	\end{proof}

268	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
269	\label{sec:ab_main}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	\leanok
274	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
275	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
276	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
277	  
278	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
279	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
282	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
283	  %  \(\)
284	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
285	  %  \(\)"
286	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
287	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
288	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
289	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
290	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
291	  \[
292	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
293	  \]
294	\end{theorem}
295	
296	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
297	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
298	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
299	%  \(\)
300	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
301	%  \(\)
302	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
303	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
304	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
305	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
306	%  Proposition REF we see that
307	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
308	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
309	%  \begin{align*}
310	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
311	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
312	%  \ldots,\\
313	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
314	%  \end{align*}
315	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
316	%  \begin{align*}
317	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
318	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
319	%  \ldots,\\
320	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
321	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
322	%  \end{align*}
323	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
324	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
325	%
326	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
327	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
328	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
329	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
330	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
331	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
332	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
333	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
334	%  \(\)
335	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
336	%  \(\)
337	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
338	%  shows that
339	%  \(\)
340	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
341	%  M/xM \to 0
342	%  \(\)
343	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
344	%  By Lemma REF we have
345	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
346	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
347	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
348	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
349	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
350	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
351	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
352	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
353	\begin{proof}
354	  
355	  
356	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
357	
358	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
359	  finite free resolution
360	  \[
361	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
362	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
363	  \]
364	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
365	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
366	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
367	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
368	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
369	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
370	  sequences
371	  \[
372	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
373	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
374	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
375	  \]
376	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
377	  yields the chain of inequalities
378	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
379	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
380	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
381	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
382	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
383	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
384	
385	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
386	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
387	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
388	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
389	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
390	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
391	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
392	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
393	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
394	  \[
395	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
396	  \]
397	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
398	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
399	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
400	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
401	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
402	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
403	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
404	  \[
405	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
406	  \]
407	  Substituting yields
408	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
409	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
410	\end{proof}

451	  \emph{inductive step} paragraph (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum},
452	  proof block), using \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one} for the
453	  depth-drop on both \(R\) and \(M\) after quotienting by an
454	  \(M\)-regular \(x \in \mathfrak m\), plus a snake-lemma carving of
455	  the minimal resolution to extract the \(R/(x)\)-minimal resolution

457	  \begin{enumerate}

459	      \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}): every finite free

470	    \item Depth drops by one

473	      \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\) and likewise on \(R\).

478	  axiom-clean (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}).

481	\begin{lemma}
482	
483	\leanok
484	  [Depth drops by one under a regular element]
485	  \label{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
486	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular}
487	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
488	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LL (\texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}).}
489	
490	  For a Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite
491	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
492	  \[
493	    \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M/xM) + 1 \;=\; \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M).
494	  \]
495	\end{lemma}
496	\begin{proof}
497	  Reduces to the Ext characterisation
498	  (\cref{lem:depth_via_ext}) on both sides; the short exact sequence
499	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{\,\cdot x\,} M \to M/xM \to 0\) gives a
500	  long exact sequence in \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) on which
501	  multiplication by \(x\) is zero (since \(x \in \text{Ann}_R \kappa\)),
502	  breaking the LES into short exact pieces
503	  \(0 \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M) \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M/xM) \to
504	  \text{Ext}^{i+1}(\kappa, M) \to 0\). The translation to the
505	  \(\mathbb N_\infty\) depth equality goes through
506	  \texttt{ENat.forall\_natCast\_le\_iff\_le}.
507	\end{proof}
508	
509	\subsection{Inductive-step substrate: per-gap decomposition of
510	  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
511	\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}
512	
513	The inductive step
514	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
515	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula is gated, as of the current pinned
516	Mathlib snapshot, by exactly three absent substrate ingredients
517	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth that is now in hand
518	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
519	decomposition that the proof block of
520	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folds into four bullets:
521	its purpose is to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
522	estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps can be formalised
523	independently (and where the dependency graph permits, in parallel).
524	
525	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
526	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
527	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
528	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
529	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
530	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
531	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
532	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
533	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
534	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
535	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
536	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
537	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
538	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
539	
540	\paragraph{Dependency diagram.} Read top-to-bottom as ``provides
541	input to'':
542	\begin{verbatim}
543	                gap (1)
544	       minimal-resolution carving
545	   (Stacks lemma-add-trivial-complex)
546	                  |
547	                  v
548	                gap (3)
549	        snake lemma on minimal
550	        resolution under x in m
551	                  |
552	                  v
553	   +--------------+--------------+
554	   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
555	   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
556	   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"
557	                  ^
558	                  |
559	                gap (4)
560	       depth drops by one (CLOSED;
561	       see lem:depth_drops_by_one)
562	\end{verbatim}
563	Gap (4) is already formalised
564	(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}, the project's
565	\texttt{depth\_quotSMulTop\_succ\_eq\_depth\_of\_isSMulRegular})
566	and feeds the closure assembly directly. Gap (2) feeds the closure
567	assembly independently of the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3) chain.
568	
569	\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
570	for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
571	the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
572	Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on
573	other gaps:
574	\begin{center}
575	  \begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|c|c|}
576	    \hline
577	    Gap & Statement                              & LOC est.  & Iters
578	        & Mathlib status     & Depends on \\
579	    \hline
580	    (1) & Minimal-resolution carving             & 40--80    & 1--2
581	        & per-syzygy step CLOSED iter-199
582	                                                                & none \\
583	    (2) & Stacks 00MF (``what is exact'')        & 150--200  & 2--3
584	        & absent             & none \\

586	        & \textbf{OBVIATED iter-200} & --- \\

592	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
593	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
594	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
595	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
596	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
597	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
598	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
599	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
600	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
601	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
602	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
603	Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof.
604	
605	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
606	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
607	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
608	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
609	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
610	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
611	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
612	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
613	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
614	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
615	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
616	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
617	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
618	
619	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
620	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
621	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
622	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
623	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
624	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
625	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
626	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
627	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
628	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
629	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
630	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
631	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
632	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
633	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
634	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
635	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
636	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
637	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
638	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
639	candidate.
640	
641	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
642	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
643	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
644	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
645	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
646	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
647	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
648	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
649	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
650	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
651	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
652	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
653	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
654	helper
655	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
656	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
657	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
658	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
659	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
660	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
661	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
662	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
663	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
664	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
665	inductive code.
666	
667	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
668	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
669	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
670	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
671	for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
672	parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
673	chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
674	\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
675	\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),
676	yielding 3--5 iters in the parallel-best case. The substrate
677	sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
678	(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be
679	dispatched as a parallel prover lane the moment a free slot opens.
680	This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget
681	recorded in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}: the refined window is somewhat
682	tighter on the upper end (5--8 vs.\ 6--12 sequential) and is
683	substantially tighter under parallel dispatch (3--5 parallel-best
684	vs.\ 6--12).

686	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
687	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
688	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}
691	
692	\leanok
693	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
694	    over a local ring]
695	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
696	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
697	  \uses{def:depth}
698	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
699	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
700	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
701	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
702	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
703	  %     \ldots
704	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
705	  %     R^{n_i}
706	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
707	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
708	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
709	  %     \ldots
710	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
711	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
712	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
713	  %  complex
714	  %     \ldots \to
715	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
716	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
717	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
718	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
719	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
720	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
723	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
724	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
725	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
726	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
727	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
728	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
729	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
730	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
731	  \begin{enumerate}
732	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
733	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
734	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
735	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
736	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
737	      summands).
738	  \end{enumerate}
739	\end{lemma}
740	\begin{proof}
741	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
742	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
743	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
744	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
745	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
746	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
747	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
748	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
749	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
750	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
751	
752	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
753	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
754	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
755	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
756	
757	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
758	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
759	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
760	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
761	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
762	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
763	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
764	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
765	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
766	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
767	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
768	\end{proof}
769	
770	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
771	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
772	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
773	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
774	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
775	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
776	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
777	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
778	is not needed.
779

902	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)

1018	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1019	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1020	
1021	\begin{definition}
1022	
1023	\leanok
1024	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1025	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1026	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1027	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1028	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1029	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1030	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1031	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1032	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1033	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1034	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1035	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1036	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1037	\end{definition}
1038	
1039	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
1040	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
1041	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
1042	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1043	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
1044	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
1045	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
1046	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
1047	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1048	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1049	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1050	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
1051	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
1052	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
1053	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
1054	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
1055	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
1056	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
1057	\begin{corollary}
1058	
1059	\leanok
1060	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1061	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1062	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1063	  
1064	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1065	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1066	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1067	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1068	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1069	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1070	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1071	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1072	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1073	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1074	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1075	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1076	\end{corollary}
1077	
1078	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1079	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1080	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1081	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1082	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1083	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1084	\begin{proof}
1085	  
1086	  
1087	  
1088	  
1089	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1090	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1091	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1092	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1093	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1094	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1095	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1096	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1097	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1098	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1099	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1100	
1101	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1102	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1103	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1104	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1105	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1106	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1107	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1108	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1109	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1110	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1111	\end{proof}
1112	
1113	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1114	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1115	
1116	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1117	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1118	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1119	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1120	
1121	\begin{enumerate}
1122	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1123	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1124	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1125	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1126	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1127	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1128	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1129	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1130	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1131	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1132	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1133	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1134	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1135	\end{enumerate}
1136	
1137	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1138	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1139	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1140	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1141	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1142	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1143
