43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
207	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
208	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
209	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
210	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
216	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
218	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
219	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
220	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
221	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
222	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
223	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
224	  \(S_M\)-module.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  The canonical comparison map
229	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
230	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
231	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
232	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
233	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
234	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
235	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
236	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
237	  the conclusion.
238	\end{proof}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	\leanok
243	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
244	  the relative dimension]
245	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
247	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
248	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
249	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
250	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
251	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
252	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
253	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
254	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
260	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
261	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
262	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
263	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
264	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
265	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
266	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
267	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
268	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
269	  ranks equal \(n\), so
270	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
271	\end{proof}

273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
342	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
343	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
344	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
345	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
346	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
347	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
348	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
349	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
350	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
351	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
352	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
353	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
355	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
356	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
357	  % $S'$-modules
358	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
359	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
360	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
361	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
362	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
363	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
364	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
365	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
366	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
367	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
369	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
370	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
371	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
372	  \[
373	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
374	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
377	  \]
378	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
379	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
380	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
381	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
382	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
383	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
384	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
385	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
386	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
387	  \[
388	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
389	  \]
390	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
391	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
392	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
393	\end{lemma}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
397	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
398	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
399	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
400	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
401	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
402	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
403	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
404	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
405	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
406	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
407	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
408	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
409	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
410	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
411	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
412	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
413	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
414	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
415	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
416	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
417	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
418	  dimension equals \(1\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
423	  \begin{enumerate}
424	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
425	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
426	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
427	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
428	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
429	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
430	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
431	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
432	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
434	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
435	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
436	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
437	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
438	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
439	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
440	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
441	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
442	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
443	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
444	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
445	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
447	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
448	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
449	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
450	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
451	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
452	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
453	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
454	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
455	      the conclusion.
456	  \end{enumerate}
457	\end{proof}

637	\paragraph{Iter-201+ Jacobian-regular-sequence witness recipe.} The
638	residual substrate of Stage 6.A is the Jacobian-criterion
639	regular-sequence witness for the relations of an
640	\(\texttt{Algebra.SubmersivePresentation}\).

798	\begin{lemma}
799	
800	\leanok
801	  [Smooth over \(\bar k\) \(\Rightarrow\) regular local stalk (Stacks 00TT)]
802	  \label{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}
803	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth}
804	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT --- Jacobian criterion
805	  % (smooth-implies-regular direction) (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex,
806	  % L38593--38611; tag verified against the Stacks website tag/00TT page,
807	  % "Lemma 10.140.3" in section 10.140 "Smooth algebras over fields").
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be any field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
809	  % Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak q \subset S$ be a prime corresponding to
810	  % $x \in X$. The following are equivalent:
811	  % \begin{enumerate}
812	  % \item The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.
813	  % \item We have
814	  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
815	  % \leq \dim_x X$.
816	  % \item We have
817	  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
818	  % = \dim_x X$.
819	  % \end{enumerate}
820	  % Moreover, in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular."

859	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.}
860	  Let \(X\) be a variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) (geometrically
861	  integral, separated, locally of finite type) and assume the structure morphism
862	  \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) is \emph{smooth}. Then for every point \(z \in X\) the
863	  stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) is a regular local ring.
864	
865	  The hypothesis \([IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]\) is load-bearing: tag 00TT is stated
866	  over an arbitrary field, but the variant Mathlib will be able to discharge
867	  axiom-clean at commit \texttt{b80f227} routes through residue fields that are
868	  \(\bar k\)-rational (every closed point of \(X\) has residue field \(\bar k\) under
869	  algebraic closure, and the chain of localisations from a closed point reaches
870	  every \(z \in X\) by the standard chain-of-primes argument). Without algebraic
871	  closure one needs the geometric-regularity refinement of tag 00TT (Stacks tag
872	  \texttt{056T} in the project's \texttt{stacks-varieties.md} card) and an extra
873	  base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step.
874	\end{lemma}

877	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.} The cited lemma packages the
878	  Jacobian-criterion equivalence and concludes ``in this case the local ring
879	  \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is regular''. Translated to the scheme level: a smooth
880	  finite-type morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) restricted to an affine chart
881	  \(\Spec(S) \subseteq X\) gives an affine algebra \(\bar k \to S\) that is
882	  smooth at every prime \(\mathfrak q \subset S\); tag 00TT then yields
883	  regularity of the localisation \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) at every
884	  point \(z \in X\) lying over \(\mathfrak q\).
885	
886	  Mathlib hooks the prover will assemble for the formalised version of this

891	  Jacobian-criterion refinement of \texttt{Module.FormallyEtale} /
892	  \texttt{Algebra.Smooth} via the cotangent complex \texttt{H\^{}1\,LSm} (or

895	  specialisation \(x \rightsquigarrow z\) whose stalk localises to
896	  \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.localRing\_at\_closed\_point}
897	  family); (iv) preservation of regularity under localisation of a regular
898	  local ring at a prime (Mathlib
899	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.localization\_isRegularLocalRing}, or the same
900	  fact phrased via Stacks tag \texttt{00OF}). The composition of (i)--(iv) is
901	  the formalised content of the smooth-implies-regular implication; the only
902	  Mathlib gap at \texttt{b80f227} is in step (ii)--(iii), specifically the
903	  Jacobian-criterion scheme-level packaging that promotes
904	  \texttt{[Smooth\,X.hom]\,[IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]} to
905	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing\,(X.left.presheaf.stalk\,z)}.
906	
907	  As a backup citation, Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Chapter~19
908	  (``Regular Rings'') develops the regular-local-ring theory needed for
909	  step~(iv); see \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} for the depth-/CM-side
910	  references the project already maintains.
911	\end{proof}

38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
214	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
215	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
216	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
217	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
218	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
219	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
220	  \[
221	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
222	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
223	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
224	  \]
225	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
226	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
227	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
228	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
229	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
230	  isomorphisms
231	  \[
232	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
233	    \qquad
234	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	  \]
236	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
237	  \[
238	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
239	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
240	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
241	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
242	  \]
243	  and a canonical braiding
244	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
245	  inherited from the corresponding data on
246	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
247	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
252	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
253	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
254	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
255	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
256	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
257	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
258	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
259	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
260	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
261	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
262	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
263	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
264	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
265	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
266	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
267	\end{proof}
268	
269	\begin{theorem}
270	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
271	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
273	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
274	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
275	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
276	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
277	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
278	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
279	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
280	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
281	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
282	\end{theorem}
283	
284	\begin{proof}
285	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
286	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
287	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
288	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
289	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
290	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
291	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
292	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
293	  identities yield global coherence identities on
294	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
295	  same affine descent argument.
296	\end{proof}
297	
298	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
299	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
300	
301	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
302	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
303	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
304	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
305	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
306	monoidal-category structure of
307	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
308	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
309	
310	\begin{lemma}
311	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
312	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
313	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
314	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
315	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
316	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
317	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
318	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
319	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
320	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
321	\end{lemma}
322	
323	\begin{proof}
324	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
325	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
326	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
327	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
328	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
329	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
330	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
331	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	[Inverse of an invertible module]
336	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
337	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
338	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
339	  bundle. The dual
340	  \[
341	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
342	  \]
343	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
344	  isomorphism
345	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
346	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
347	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
348	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
349	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
350	\end{lemma}
351	
352	\begin{proof}
353	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
354	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
355	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
356	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
357	  The contraction map
358	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
359	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
360	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
361	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
362	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
363	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
364	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\begin{lemma}
368	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
369	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
370	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
371	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
372	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
373	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
374	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
375	  \[
376	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
377	    \;\;=\;\;
378	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
379	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
380	  \]
381	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
382	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
383	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
384	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
385	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
386	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
387	\end{lemma}
388	
389	\begin{proof}
390	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
391	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
392	  Direct from
393	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
394	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
395	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
396	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
397	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
398	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
399	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
400	  monoidal-category structure of
401	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
402	  the same affine descent argument.
403	\end{proof}
404	
405	\begin{lemma}
406	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
407	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
408	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
409	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
410	  functor
411	  \[
412	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
413	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
414	  \]
415	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
416	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
417	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
418	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
419	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
420	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
421	  canonical isomorphism
422	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
423	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
425	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
426	\end{lemma}
427	
428	\begin{proof}
429	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
430	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
431	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
432	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
433	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
434	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
435	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
436	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
437	  same descent argument as in
438	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
439	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
440	  same cover.
441	\end{proof}
442	
443	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
444	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
445	
446	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
447	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
448	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
449	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
450	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
451	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
452	
453	\begin{theorem}
454	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
455	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
456	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
457	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
458	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
459	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
460	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
461	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
462	
463	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
464	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
465	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
466	  % groups)
467	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
468	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
469	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
470	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
471	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
472	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
473	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
474	  % \end{dfn}
475	  %  [\dots]
476	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
477	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
478	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
479	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
480	  % topologies  by
481	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
482	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
483	  %  \end{dfn}"
484	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
485	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
486	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
487	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
488	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
489	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
490	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
491	  \[
492	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
493	  \]
494	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
495	  abelian-group structure with addition
496	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
497	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
498	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
499	  inverse
500	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
501	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
502	  supplied by the substrate of
503	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
504	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
505	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
506	  map
507	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
508	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
509	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
510	\end{theorem}
511	
512	\begin{proof}
513	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
514	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
515	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
516	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
517	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
518	  By
519	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
520	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
521	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
522	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
523	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
524	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
525	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
526	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
527	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
528	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
529	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
530	
531	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
532	  functor
533	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
534	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
535	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
536	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
537	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
538	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
539	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
540	
541	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
542	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
543	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
544	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
545	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
546	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
547	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
548	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
549	  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
550	  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
551	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
552	\end{proof}
553

556	
557	The substrate splits into three logical pieces of roughly comparable
558	size; each is one prover lane in the iter-201+ schedule.

560	\paragraph{Piece 1: definition + functoriality
561	(\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}).}
562	The Lean target file is the new
563	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. The body
564	consists of:
565	\begin{itemize}
566	  \item a definition
567	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} taking two
568	    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) objects and producing one, via
569	    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) on the underlying
570	    presheaves and the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site;
571	  \item the functorial action on morphisms (bifunctoriality, identity

579	20$--$30$ LOC for bifunctoriality, $\approx 30$--$40$ LOC for the

584	1. The body consists of:

589	  \item assembling the data into a
590	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory} instance with target

681	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the

684	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
685	    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is

688	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-\(0\) curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	\label{chap:avr_for_rr}
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/ChartIso.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityLemma.lean
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
10	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
11	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
12	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
13	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
14	% keystone directly). The headline prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
15	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
16	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
17	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
18	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
19	The genus-\(0\) arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
20	(\(J = \Spec k\)); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
21	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\)
22	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
23	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field \(k\), so the
24	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
25	O(-2)) = 0\(, hence \)df = 0\(, hence \)f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
26	characteristic-\(p\) Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant \(f\) can have \(df = 0\). The
27	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
28	The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
29	Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
30	argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
31	for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
32	derives the genus-\(0\) base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube --- and, in this
33	project's reading, \emph{without} Milne's Theorem~3.2 (rational-map extension), Lemma~3.3,
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials (the ``4th route''; see
35	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). The decisive observation is that \(\mathbb P^1\) is
36	\emph{already complete} and \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is \emph{already a total morphism}, so
37	there is no rational defect map to extend over a complete surface and Theorem~3.2 never enters.
38	Instead the base case is closed by the \textbf{\(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut} (the project's
39	primary route, an Archon-original realisation of Milne Proposition~3.9): one uses the
40	\emph{multiplicative scaling} action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
41	\mathbb P^1\(, \)(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\( (the M\"obius scaling fixing both \)0$ and
42	\(\infty\)), which is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb
43	G_m\( (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). Normalising \)f(0) = \eta_A\( and composing \)h := \sigma_\times
44	\fatsemi f\(, one applies additivity of \)\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
45	(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires
46	only the \emph{first} factor complete --- with \(V = \mathbb P^1\) proper, \(W = \mathbb G_m\), and
47	base points \(0 \in \mathbb P^1\), \(1 \in \mathbb G_m\). Because \(0\) is a \emph{fixed point} of
48	scaling, the \(W\)-axis restriction \(\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0)\) is
49	\emph{constant} at \(\eta_A\), so the decomposition collapses to \(h = p \fatsemi f\), i.e.\
50	\(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\). Specialising \(x = 1\) gives that \(f|_{\mathbb G_m}\) is constant, and since
51	\(\mathbb G_m\) is dense in \(\mathbb P^1\) with \(A\) separated, \(f\) is constant
52	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, via the separated-target/dominant-source handle
53	\cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}). This route uses \emph{only} the proven Corollary~1.5, a
54	scaling fixed point, and density --- it never forms the additive defect map, never invokes
55	\(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\), and so sidesteps the ``image of a group homomorphism is a
56	closed subgroup'' input that has no Mathlib support. The classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive
57	argument (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} via the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) incompatibility
58	\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, the heart of Milne Proposition~3.9) is retained only as the
59	Milne-faithful alternative and is \emph{off} the genus-\(0\) critical path. The corollary that a
60	pointed regular map of abelian
61	varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne Corollary~1.2) is recorded
62	for completeness and for Route~A but is \emph{not} needed on the genus-\(0\) path; Milne's
63	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is likewise demoted to a Route-A-only item
64	(the Albanese universal property), no longer on the genus-\(0\) critical path
65	(\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). Combining these
66	gives that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant
67	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-\(0\)
68	curves (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}, \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) over \(\bar k\)) yields the
69	headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
70	\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
71	neither Serre duality nor the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is
72	fully decoupled from Route~A.
73	\medskip
74	Throughout, \(\bar k\) is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
75	\emph{abelian variety} over \(\bar k\) is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
76	project's Lean encoding \(A\) is an object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) carrying
77	\(\mathtt{GrpObj}\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{Smooth}\), and \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\).
78	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
79	\begin{lemma}
80	\leanok
81	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
82	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
83	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
84	  
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
86	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
88	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
89	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
90	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
91	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
92	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
93	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety, and let \(Y\), \(Z\) be any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
94	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism such that for some \(y_0 \in Y\) the image
95	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0 \in Z\). Then there is a morphism
96	  \(g \colon Y \to Z\) with \(f = g \circ p_2\), where \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
97	  projection; that is, \(f\) depends only on the \(Y\)-coordinate, and \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) for any
98	  fixed \(x_0 \in X\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
101	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
102	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
103	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
104	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
105	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
106	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
107	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
108	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
109	% assertion."
110	\begin{proof}
111	\leanok
112	% NOTE (iter-159 review): the iter-157 unsoundness REMAINS REPAIRED. The collapse hypothesis
113	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}) is threaded through all three helpers (rigidity_lemma ->
114	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
115	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
116	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
117	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the
118	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).
119	% NOTE (iter-160): the \uses edges run FORWARD along the true Lean dependency order
120	% (rigidity_lemma -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open -> rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine), so the
121	% not-proven status of the residual sorry propagates up and de-launders BOTH thm:rigidity_lemma and
122	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.
123	% NOTE (iter-161): bridge 2 (rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine) was DECOMPOSED into two named
124	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:
125	%   - Step 2, morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints: "two morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a
126	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
127	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
128	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
129	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
130	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
131	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
132	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
133	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
134	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
135	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
136	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
137	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.
138	  Fix any point \(x_0 \in X\) and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\) (the
139	  composite of \(y \mapsto (x_0, y)\) with \(f\)). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety (in
140	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
141	  the equality of morphisms \(f = g \circ p_2\) it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
142	  open subset of \(X \times Y\).
143	  Let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U\) (a closed set),
144	  and set \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection
145	  \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
146	  completeness/properness); hence \(G\), the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\), is closed in
147	  \(Y\). The hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U\) gives
148	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), so \(y_0 \notin G\). Therefore
149	  \(V := Y - G\) is a non-empty open subset of \(Y\).
150	  For each \(y \in V\) we have \(X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing\), i.e.
151	  \(f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U\). Thus the complete (proper) variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is
152	  mapped by \(f\) into the \emph{affine} variety \(U\). A proper connected variety mapping to an
153	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
154	  variety is constant). Hence \(f(X \times \{y\})\) is one point, necessarily
155	  \(f(x_0, y) = g(y)\). So for all \(x \in X\) and \(y \in V\),
156	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
157	  The two morphisms \(f\) and \(g \circ p_2\) thus agree on the non-empty open
158	  \(X \times V\), which is dense in the irreducible \(X \times Y\); as \(Z\) is separated they
159	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
160	\end{proof}
161	\begin{remark}
162	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
163	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of \(X\) makes
164	  \(p_2\) a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
165	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
166	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
167	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
168	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
169	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring \(V\) complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically
170	  irreducible.)
171	\end{remark}
172	\begin{remark}
173	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
174	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
175	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
176	  collapsed map \((x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\) followed by \(f\) with \(g \circ p_2\));
177	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
178	  reduced \(X \times Y\) into the separated \(Z\) that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
179	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
180	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
181	  open \(X \times V\) and the slice-constancy of \(f\) on it). The collapse hypothesis
182	  \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\) must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
183	  what makes \(V\) non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
184	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).
185	  \emph{Status (iter-161).} Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) is \textbf{built} as the axiom-clean
186	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-\(y_0\) fact is \textbf{closed} (via the
187	  coarse \texttt{PullbackCarrier} layer), so \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open} is
188	  \texttt{sorry}-free in its own body. Bridge~2 (slice-constancy on the saturated open \(U\),
189	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}) has now been \textbf{decomposed} along its
190	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
191	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
192	  Jacobson source \(\Rightarrow\) equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
193	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice
194	  argument) is the chain's \emph{single genuinely-deep residual} \texttt{sorry}. The body of
195	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).
196	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) STALE as of iter-162 — Step~1 is now PROVEN and the WHOLE
197	  % Rigidity-Lemma chain (thm:rigidity_lemma … lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine,
198	  % via the new helper lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral) is sorry-free + axiom-clean
199	  % ({propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}, re-verified). The "single genuinely-deep residual
200	  % sorry" wording here and in the proof blocks of lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open / …saturated… /
201	  % …eqAt_closedPoint… should be refreshed by the plan/blueprint-writer to "chain closed iter-162".
202	  This route requires the base field to be algebraically closed \emph{and} the source to be of finite
203	  type: Step~2 needs the closed points of \(U\) to be dense (the Jacobson-space property), which over
204	  the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) holds because \(X \times Y\) is locally of finite type.
