1095	group-scheme refinement. The following downstream constructions live in
1096	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
1097	\begin{itemize}
1098	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3) --- the open and
1099	    closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the unit and
1100	    consisting of those classes lying in the connected component of
1101	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\). Its dimension, smoothness, and abelian-variety
1102	    structure are the content of the A.3 chapter
1103	    \texttt{Picard\_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex} (planned), gated on this
1104	    chapter.
1105	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme representability} --- consumed by
1106	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} via \texttt{thm:quot\_representable}
1107	    (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), not re-proved here.
1108	  \item \textbf{\'Etale sheafification of \(\Pic^\sharp\)} ---
1109	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) produces
1110	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) from the set-valued presheaf of
1111	    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}; this chapter consumes the
1112	    sheaf as a black box.
1113	  \item \textbf{Universal Poincar\'e sheaf} (Kleiman~\S 4 Ex.~ex:univshf)
1114	    --- exists when \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (as opposed to
1115	    its \'etale sheafification) and \(C/k\) has a section; out of scope
1116	    here. The Albanese / Abel--Jacobi consumption in
1117	    \cref{chap:AbelJacobi} works with the \'etale-sheafified
1118	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) representation and does not require the
1119	    Poincar\'e sheaf.
1120	  \item \textbf{Mumford's example} (Kleiman~\S 4 eg:Mumford), counter-examples
1121	    to representability for non-integral geometric fibers --- not
1122	    relevant to a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
147	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
148	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
149	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
150	  \[
151	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
152	  \]
153	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
154	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
155	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{theorem}
159	
160	\leanok
161	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
162	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
164	  
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
166	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
169	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
170	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
171	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
172	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
174	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
175	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
176	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
177	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
178	\end{theorem}
179	
180	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
181	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
182	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
183	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
184	% Theorem~REF by restricting to a non-empty
185	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
186	\begin{proof}
187	  
188	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
189	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
190	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
191	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). Theorem~REF
192	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
193	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
194	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
195	\end{proof}
196	
197	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
198	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
199	
200	\begin{theorem}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
204	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
206	  
207	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
208	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
209	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
210	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
212	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and let
213	  %  \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on the projective space \(\PP^n_S\)
214	  %  over \(S\). Then the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials of
215	  %  restrictions of \(\F\) to fibers of \(\PP^n_S\to S\)
216	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each \(f\in I\) there exist
217	  %  a locally closed subscheme \(S_f\) of \(S\),
218	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
219	  %
220	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set \(|S_f|\) of \(S_f\) consists of
221	  %  all points \(s\in S\) where the Hilbert polynomial of the
222	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_s\) is \(f\). In particular,
223	  %  the subsets \(|S_f|\subset |S|\) are disjoint,
224	  %  and their set-theoretic union is \(|S|\).
225	  %
226	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
227	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
228	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
229	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
230	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
231	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
232	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
233	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
234	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\).
235	  %
236	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
237	  %  Let the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
238	  %  ordering, defined by putting \(f<g\) whenever \(f(n) < g(n)\) for all
239	  %  \(n \gg  0\). Then the closure in \(S\) of the subset \(|S_f|\)
240	  %  is contained in the union of all \(|S_g|\) where \(f\le g\)."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
242	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
243	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on the relative
244	  projective space \(\PP^n_S\) over \(S\).  Then:
245	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
246	    \item The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
247	      \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is
248	      finite.
249	    \item For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme
250	      \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly
251	      those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\).  The collection \(\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}\) is
252	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\).  Forming the coproduct
253	      \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled
254	      from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \F\) on
255	      \(\PP^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this
256	      property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi^* \F\) on
257	      \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through \(i\).
258	      Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
259	    \item Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the
260	      closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
261	  \end{enumerate}
262	\end{theorem}
263	
264	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
265	the special case \(n = 0\) (when \(\PP^n_S = S\) and \(\F\) is just a coherent sheaf
266	on \(S\)), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
267	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
268	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
269	of \(\F(m)\) for \(m \gg 0\) on \(\F_{V_i}\) for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
270	isomorphisms for the direct images \(\pi_* \F(m)\), which allow comparing
271	strata for different twists.
272	
273	\begin{lemma}
274	[Flat locus on \(S\) for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
275	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
276	  
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
278	  % \(n=0\) (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
279	  % L1849-L1885).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "\textbf{Special case: } Let \(n=0\), so that \(\PP^n_S=S\).
282	  %  For any \(s\in S\), the \textbf{fiber} \(\F|_s\) of \(\F\) over \(s\)
283	  %  will mean the pull-back of \(\F\) to the subscheme
284	  %  \(\spec \kappa(s)\) [...]. The Hilbert polynomial of the
285	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to the fiber over \(s\) is the degree \(0\)
286	  %  polynomial \(e \in \Q[\lambda]\), where \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s\).
287	  %
288	  %  By Nakayama lemma, any basis of \(\F|_s\) prolongs to a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\)
289	  %  to give a set of generators for \(F|_U\). Repeating this argument,
290	  %  we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) in which
291	  %  there is a right-exact sequence
292	  %  ${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}
293	  %  \stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$.
294	  %  Let \(I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V\) be the ideal sheaf
295	  %  formed by the entries of the \(e\times m\) matrix
296	  %  \((\psi_{i,j})\) [...].
297	  %  Let \(V_e\) be the closed subscheme of \(V\) defined by \(I_e\).
298	  %  For any morphism of schemes
299	  %  \(f: T\to V\), the pull-back sequence [...]
300	  %  is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products.
301	  %  Hence the pull-back \(f^*\F\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module of
302	  %  rank \(e\) if and only if \(f^*\psi =0\), that is,
303	  %  \(f\) factors via the subscheme \(V_e\hra V\) defined by the vanishing of all
304	  %  entries \(\psi_{i,j}\).
305	  %  Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
306	  %
307	  %  As the rank of the matrix \((\psi_{i,j})\) is lower semi-continuous,
308	  %  it follows that the function \(e\) is upper semi-continuous, which
309	  %  proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
310	  %  when \(n=0\)."
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (special case \(n=0\) of the main theorem).}
312	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(\F\) be a coherent \(\OO_S\)-module.
313	  For each integer \(e \ge 0\), there exists a locally-closed subscheme
314	  \(S_e \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set is
315	  \(\{s \in S : \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s = e\}\). The family \(\{S_e\}_e\) is a
316	  finite, mutually disjoint, set-theoretically covering stratification of \(S\),
317	  and a morphism \(f : T \to S\) pulls back \(\F\) to a locally-free
318	  \(\OO_T\)-module of rank \(e\) if and only if \(f\) factors through \(S_e\).  The
319	  function \(s \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\) is upper-semicontinuous on \(S\).
320	\end{lemma}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  
324	  By [Nitsure], \S4 (verbatim above): for \(s \in S\), Nakayama produces a
325	  neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a right-exact local presentation
326	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\psi} \OO_V^{\oplus e} \to \F|_V \to 0\) where
327	  \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\).  The closed subscheme \(V_e \hookrightarrow V\)
328	  defined by the ideal sheaf generated by the matrix entries of \(\psi\)
329	  represents the locus where \(\F\) becomes locally free of rank \(e\): by
330	  right-exactness of tensor product, a base change \(f : T \to V\) has
331	  \(f^* \F\) locally free of rank \(e\) iff \(f^* \psi = 0\), iff \(f\) factors
332	  through \(V_e\).  Glueing the \(V_e\) over the cover of \(S\) by such
333	  neighbourhoods (and intersecting with \(V \setminus \bigcup_{e' > e} V_{e'}\)
334	  to get the strictly-equal-to-\(e\) stratum) produces the locally-closed
335	  \(S_e\). Upper-semicontinuity of \(e\) follows because the rank of \(\psi\) is
336	  lower-semicontinuous.
337	\end{proof}
338	
339	\begin{lemma}
340	[Noetherian induction reduction to the flat case]
341	  \label{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', General case
344	  % opening paragraphs (read from

368	  %  finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
369	  %  \(V_i\) of \(S\) such that set-theoretically
370	  %  \(|S|\) is the union of the \(|V_i|\) and
371	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_{V_i}\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_{V_i}\).
372	  %  As each \(V_i\) is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
373	  %  are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows
374	  %  that only finitely many polynomials occur in \(V_i\)
375	  %  in the family of Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\)
376	  %  as \(s\) varies over points of \(V_i\). This allows us to conclude
377	  %  the following:
378	  %
379	  %  \textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials
380	  %  \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\) occur, as \(s\)
381	  %  varies over all of \(S\)."
382	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (general case, reduction by Noetherian induction).}
383	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) the relative projective
384	  space, and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on \(\PP^n_S\).  Then there exist finitely many
385	  reduced, locally-closed, pairwise disjoint subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\)
386	  whose set-theoretic union is \(|S|\) and such that \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is
387	  flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) for each \(i\). Consequently, only finitely many
388	  Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) occur as \(s\) varies
389	  over \(S\).
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  
394	  By [Nitsure], \S4: \(S\) being noetherian, decompose \(|S| = \bigcup Y_j\) into
395	  finitely many irreducible components, each closed.  For an irreducible
396	  component \(Y\), let \(U \subseteq Y\) be the open subset not lying on any other
397	  component, given the reduced subscheme structure (an integral, locally-closed
398	  subscheme of \(S\)).  By Theorem~REF applied to
399	  \(\F|_{\PP^n_U}\), there is a non-empty open \(V \subseteq U\) over which \(\F\) is
400	  flat.  Replace \(S\) by the reduced closed complement \(S \setminus V\) and
401	  repeat. Noetherianity of \(S\) terminates the induction in finitely many
402	  steps, producing the \(V_i\).  On each \(V_i\) the Hilbert polynomial is locally
403	  constant (flat families have locally constant Hilbert polynomial), hence
404	  takes finitely many values on the noetherian (hence quasi-compact) scheme
405	  \(V_i\); the finite union of finite sets is finite.
406	\end{proof}
407	
408	\begin{lemma}
409	[Assembly of the strata by Noetherian induction]
410	  \label{lem:noetherian_induction_strata}
411	  
412	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Assembly via
413	  % direct images \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\) (read from
414	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1992-L2090).
415	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
416	  % "Let \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) denote the projection. Consider the
417	  %  coherent sheaves \(E_0, \ldots, E_n\) on \(S\), defined by
418	  %  \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.\)
419	  %  By applying the special case of of the theorem (where the relative dimension
420	  %  \(n\) of \(\PP^n_S\) is \(0\)) to the sheaf \(E_0\) on \(\PP^0_S=S\), we get
421	  %  a stratification \((W_{e_0})\) of \(S\)
422	  %  indexed by integers \(e_0\), such that for any morphism
423	  %  \(f: T\to S\) the pull-back \(f^*E_0\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module
424	  %  of rank \(e_0\) if and only if \(f\) factors via \(W_{e_0} \hra S\).
425	  %  Next, for each stratum \(W_{e_0}\), we take the
426	  %  flattening stratification \((W_{e_0,e_1})\) for \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\), and so on.
427	  %  Thus in \(n+1\) steps, we obtain finitely many
428	  %  locally closed subschemes
429	  %  \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \subset S\)
430	  %  such that for any morphism \(f:T\to S\) the pull-back
431	  %  \(f^*E_i\) for \(i=0,\ldots,n\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-modules of
432	  %  of constant rank \(e_i\) if and only if \(f\) factors
433	  %  via \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \hra S\). [...]
434	  %  This shows that at any point \(s\in W_f\), the Hilbert polynomial
435	  %  \(P_s(m)\) equals \(f\). The desired locally closed subscheme \(S_f \subset S\),
436	  %  whose existence is asserted by the theorem,
437	  %  will turn out to be a certain closed subscheme \(S_f \subset W_f\) whose
438	  %  underlying subset is all of \(|W_f|\). [...]
439	  %  As \(S_f\) is the flattening stratification for \(\pi_*\F(p)\),
440	  %  the property (iii) of the theorem follows from the corresponding property in
441	  %  the case \(n=0\), applied to the sheaf \(\pi_*\F(p)\) on \(S\)."
442	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (assembly step combining the \(n=0\) special case
443	  applied to direct images \(\pi_*\F(N+i)\)).}
444	  Let \(S\), \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\), \(\F\) be as in
445	  Theorem~REF.  There exists an integer
446	  \(N \ge 0\) such that for every \(s \in S\) the higher cohomologies
447	  \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) vanish for all \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N\), and the base-change
448	  homomorphism \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism
449	  for \(m \ge N\), \(s \in S\).  Iterating the \(n=0\) stratification
450	  (Lemma~REF) on the coherent sheaves \(E_i := \pi_* \F(N+i)\)
451	  for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) produces a finite collection of locally-closed
452	  subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\) indexed by tuples
453	  \((e_0, \dots, e_n) \in \Z^{n+1}\). Re-indexing each tuple by the unique
454	  numerical polynomial \(f \in \Q[\lambda]\) of degree \(\le n\) with
455	  \(f(N+i) = e_i\) gives a finite stratification \(\{W_f\}\) of \(S\), and the
456	  locally-closed subschemes \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) of
457	  Theorem~REF are obtained by further
458	  intersecting with the closed-subscheme conditions that
459	  \(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) be locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all \(i \ge 0\)
460	  (terminating by Noetherianity).
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: full proof is the long body of [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem
464	% ``flattening stratification'', spanning L1844-L2090 (~250 lines: special
465	% case, Noetherian induction, statements (A)/(B)/(C), then the assembly via
466	% direct images and the closed-subscheme construction inside each \(W_f\)).
467	% Verbatim retrieved per-sub-lemma above; the structural restatement below in
468	% project notation is the canonical Nitsure-style restated proof.
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  By Lemma~REF, the family of Hilbert polynomials
472	  \(\{P_s\}\) on \(S\) is finite, and there is a finite stratification
473	  \(\{V_i\}\) of \(S\) on each of which \(\F\) is flat with locally-constant
474	  Hilbert polynomial.  Apply semi-continuity (cohomology and base change,
475	  [Hartshorne], III.12 / [Stacks Project], tag 02KH for the \(H^0\)-version) on
476	  the noetherian \(V_i\) to obtain a uniform integer \(N_1\) with
477	  \(R^r \pi_* \F(m) = 0\) for \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\), and \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m)) = 0\)
478	  for all \(s \in S\), \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\) (statement (B) of Nitsure's proof).
479	  Choose \(r_i \ge N_1\) on each \(V_i\) such that the base-change comparison
480	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_{V_i} \to (\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m)\) is an isomorphism for
481	  \(m \ge r_i\); composing with the cohomology-and-base-change isomorphism
482	  \(((\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m))|_s \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) for
483	  the flat family on \(V_i\) yields uniform \(N = \max r_i\) such that
484	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism for \(m \ge N\)
485	  and all \(s \in S\) (statement (C)).
486	
487	  Apply Lemma~REF sequentially to the coherent sheaves
488	  \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\): \(E_0\) produces a
489	  stratification \(\{W_{e_0}\}\) of \(S\); on each \(W_{e_0}\) apply the lemma to
490	  \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\) to refine to \(\{W_{e_0, e_1}\}\), and continue. After \(n+1\)
491	  steps the \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n}\) form a finite locally-closed stratification
492	  of \(S\) universal for the property ``each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free
493	  \(\OO_T\)-module of constant rank \(e_i\)''.  Re-index \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
494	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\) to get
495	  the family \(\{W_f\}\) indexed by polynomials.  At any \(s \in W_f\), statement
496	  (C) and vanishing (B) force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has degree \(\le n\) and is
497	  determined by its values at \(N, N+1, \dots, N+n\)).
498	
499	  The Hilbert-polynomial-coincidence locus \(|W_f|\) may have non-flat scheme
500	  structure; refine to \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) by intersecting with the
501	  closed-subscheme conditions ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally-free of rank
502	  \(f(N+i)\)'' for all \(i \ge 0\).  These conditions are cut out by an increasing
503	  chain of coherent ideal sheaves \(I_0 \subseteq I_0 + I_1 \subseteq \cdots\) on
504	  \(W_f\); Noetherianity terminates the chain in finitely many steps, defining a
505	  coherent ideal sheaf \(I\) and a closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) with
506	  \(|S_f| = |W_f|\) on which \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) is locally-free of rank \(f(N+i)\)
507	  for all \(i \ge 0\). The base-change-without-flatness lemma (cf.\ [Nitsure],
508	  \S3) yields some \(N' \ge N\) such that for \(i \ge N'\),
509	  \((\pi_* \F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism, hence
510	  the graded \(A\)-module \(\bigoplus_{i \ge N'} (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is
511	  locally-free on \(S_f\), hence \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\). The
512	  closure-of-strata property (iii) reduces to the corresponding property in
513	  the \(n=0\) case for \(\pi_* \F(p)\) on \(S\) at any \(p \ge N\) separating all
514	  Hilbert polynomials.
515	\end{proof}

517	\section{Universal property of the strata}
518	\label{sec:flatstrat_universal}
519	
520	\begin{theorem}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Universal property of the flattening stratification]
524	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_universal}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal}
526	  
527	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', part (ii)
528	  % universal property
529	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1826-L1834).
530	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
531	  % "\textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
532	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
533	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
534	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
535	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
536	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
537	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
538	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
539	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\)."
540	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf.\ [Stacks
541	  Project], tag 052H.}
542	  Let \(S\), \(\F\), and \(\{S_f\}_{f \in I}\) be as in
543	  Theorem~REF.  Set
544	  \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) with the coproduct morphism
545	  \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then \(i^* \F\)
546	  is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism
547	  \(\varphi : T \to S\) of noetherian schemes (one may take \(T\) arbitrary, the
548	  Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback
549	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi\) factors
550	  uniquely through \(i\). In particular each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
551	\end{theorem}
552	
553	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that the morphism \(\coprod_f S_f \,\to \,S\)
554	% indeed has the property (ii) of the theorem.
555	% By Lemma REF,
556	% there exists some \(N' \ge N\) such that for all \(i\ge N'\),
557	% the base-change
558	% \((\pi_*\F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_*\F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism for
559	% each \(S_f\). Therefore
560	% \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\) by Lemma REF,
561	% as the direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N'\) are locally
562	% free over \(S_f\). Conversely, if \(\phi : T\to S\) is a morphism such
563	% that \(\F_T\) is flat, then the Hilbert polynomial is locally
564	% constant over \(T\). Let \(T_f\) be the open and closed subscheme of \(T\)
565	% where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\). Clearly, the set
566	% map \(|T_f| \to |S|\) factors via \(|S_f|\). But as the direct images
567	% \({\pi_T}_*\F_T(i)\) are locally free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\), it
568	% follows in fact that the schematic morphism \(T_f \to S\) factors
569	% via \(S_f\), proving the property (ii) of the theorem."
570	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2065-L2082.)
571	\begin{proof}
572	  
573	  Flatness of \(i^* \F\) over \(S'\) was established at the end of the proof of
574	  Theorem~REF (the
575	  base-change-without-flatness lemma plus the graded-module-flatness criterion
576	  applied to the coherent sheaves \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) on each \(S_f\)).
577	  Conversely, if \(\varphi : T \to S\) satisfies \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is
578	  \(T\)-flat, then \(T\)-flatness of the family forces the Hilbert polynomial to be
579	  locally constant on \(T\).  Decompose \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed
580	  subschemes where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\).  The set map \(|T_f| \to |S|\)
581	  factors through \(|S_f|\); further, since the direct images
582	  \((\pi_T)_* \varphi^* \F(i)\) are locally-free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\) for $i
583	  \ge N'\(, the universal property of \)S_f \subseteq W_f$ as the closed
584	  subscheme cut out by these local-freeness conditions
585	  (Lemma~REF) yields a unique scheme
586	  morphism \(T_f \to S_f\) extending the set map. Assembling over \(f\) gives the
587	  unique factorisation \(\varphi = i \circ \tilde \varphi\).
588	\end{proof}

590	\section{Application: relative curves}
591	\label{sec:flatstrat_curve_application}
592	
593	In the Route~A consumer setting, the input to the flattening-stratification
594	machinery is the pullback \(C \times_k T \to T\) where \(C\) is a smooth proper
595	curve over a field \(k\) and \(T\) is any \(k\)-scheme.  The hypotheses of
596	Nitsure's Theorem~REF demand
597	\emph{projective} relative dimension, not merely \emph{proper}; we record that
598	no extra work is required to instantiate the theorem in our setting.
599	
600	\begin{lemma}
601	[Smooth proper curve over \(k\) is projective]
602	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}
603	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6 (curve \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-projective;
604	  % proper \(\Rightarrow\) projective for curves), and standard consequences in
605	  % chapter III. The precise statement ``every smooth proper curve over a field
606	  % is projective'' is one of the classical equivalences for \(\dim 1\) and is
607	  % textbook material; this block is project-bespoke (no verbatim source
608	  % quote is reproduced here as the result is folklore-level and cited only
609	  % structurally for the Route~A reduction).
610	  \textit{Source: classical; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.6.7 \& II.7.6 (curves).}
611	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\).  Then \(C \to \Spec k\) is
612	  projective.  Consequently, for any noetherian \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the relative
613	  curve \(C \times_k T \to T\) is projective in the sense of Grothendieck (there
614	  exists a relatively very ample line bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\)).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{proof}
618	  Projectivity of a smooth proper curve over a field is classical
619	  ([Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6; alternatively: every divisor of large
620	  positive degree on a smooth proper curve \(C\) is very ample, providing the
621	  embedding \(C \hookrightarrow \PP^N_k\) for some \(N\)). Pulling the ample line
622	  bundle back along \(C \times_k T \to C\) produces a relatively very ample line
623	  bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\).
624	\end{proof}
625	
626	\begin{corollary}
627	[Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative curve]
628	  \label{cor:flattening_stratification_curve}
629	  
630	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a smooth proper curve over \(k\), and \(T\) a noetherian
631	  \(k\)-scheme.  Let \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(C \times_k T\). Then the
632	  flattening-stratification conclusion of
633	  Theorem~REF applies: there is a finite
634	  set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials and locally-closed subschemes
635	  \(T_f \hookrightarrow T\) for \(f \in I\), set-theoretically covering \(T\)
636	  disjointly, such that (a) \(\F|_{C \times_k T_f}\) is flat over \(T_f\) for
637	  each \(f\), and (b) any morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) such that
638	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(C \times_k T'\) is \(T'\)-flat factors uniquely through some
639	  \(T_f\).
640	\end{corollary}
641	
642	\begin{proof}
643	  
644	  By Lemma~REF, \(C \times_k T \to T\) is
645	  projective, hence factors through a closed immersion into \(\PP^n_T\) for some
646	  \(n \ge 0\).  Push \(\F\) forward along the closed immersion (extending by zero
647	  outside the image) to a coherent sheaf \(\tilde \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\); this
648	  operation preserves \(T\)-flatness in both directions because the closed
649	  immersion is a finite morphism.  Apply
650	  Theorem~REF to \(\tilde \F\) on
651	  \(\PP^n_T\) to obtain \(\{T_f\}\) with the stated universal property.
652	\end{proof}

655	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
656	
657	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides:
658	\begin{itemize}

660	    module-theoretic notion of flatness, applicable affine-locally to the
661	    stalks \(\F_x\).
662	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat} ---
663	    \texttt{IsFlat} as a morphism property and basic stability lemmas; this is
664	    flatness of the structure morphism, \emph{not} flatness of a quasi-coherent
665	    module over the base.

667	    consumes it.

676	    \(S\)-scheme (Definition~REF). This is the
677	    sheaf-level lift of \texttt{Module.Flat} via the affine-local
678	    characterization in the second paragraph of the definition.

680	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
681	    (Theorem~REF) verbatim from

691	  \item The relative-curve specialisation

720	\section{Out of scope}
721	\label{sec:flatstrat_out_of_scope}
722	
723	This chapter packages \emph{only} the flattening stratification of a coherent
724	sheaf on a projective morphism, plus the relative-curve specialisation. The
725	following downstream / parallel constructions live in separate sibling
726	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
727	\begin{itemize}
728	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity} (Nitsure~\S2) --- input to the
729	    Quot construction, used implicitly inside the proof of
730	    Lemma~REF as the assertion that the
731	    Hilbert function stabilises beyond a uniform \(N\).  Its own blueprint home
732	    is the planned chapter \texttt{Picard\_CastelnuovoMumford.tex} (not yet
733	    scaffolded).
734	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme construction} (Nitsure~\S5, A.2.b) --- consumer of

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}
94	
95	% The four conclusions of Kleiman \S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) are split below
96	% into per-conclusion theorem blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin to
97	% a Lean declaration. The verbatim source quote of lem:agps~(3) is
98	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} below
99	% (covering all four conclusions); the three subsequent blocks reference
100	% that quote rather than duplicating it. The combined proof, sharing the
101	% \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment from Kleiman's proof, follows
102	% the fourth block.
103	
104	\begin{theorem}\leanok
105	[Identity component is a clopen subscheme]
106	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
108	
109	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
110	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
111	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
114	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
115	  % extending \(k\)."
116	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
118	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
119	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
120	  subscheme of \(G\): the inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is both an
121	  open immersion and a closed immersion of schemes.
122	\end{theorem}
123	
124	\begin{theorem}
125	
126	\leanok
127	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
128	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
129	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
130	
131	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
132	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
133	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
134	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
135	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
136	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
137	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
138	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
139	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
140	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
141	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
142	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over
143	  \(\Spec k\) is compatible with the group laws on source and target.
144	\end{theorem}
145	
146	\begin{theorem}
147	
148	\leanok
149	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
150	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
152	
153	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
154	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
155	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
156	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
157	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
158	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
159	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
160	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
161	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
162	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
163	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
164	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
165	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.
166	\end{theorem}
167	
168	\begin{theorem}
169	
170	\leanok
171	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
172	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
174	
175	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
176	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
177	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
178	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
179	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
180	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
181	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
182	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
183	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
184	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
185	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
186	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
187	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
188	\end{theorem}

191	% (the iff-direction of "connected + k-rational section ⟹ geometrically
192	% connected") as a FIRST-CLASS blueprint obligation. iter-189..iter-193
193	% Route A.3.i was STUCK because the Lean side `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section`
194	% helper was a substrate footnote without its own blueprint lemma + proof
195	% sketch. Below we add the standalone Stacks 037Q lemma block.
196	
197	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The corresponding Lean
198	% `identityComponent_geometricallyConnected` derivation at
199	% `Picard/IdentityComponent.lean:500-567` was DEMOTED from `private
200	% instance` to `private theorem` per the iter-193 lean-auditor must-fix:
201	% the prior `instance` form would silently propagate `sorryAx` through
202	% typeclass synthesis for `[GeometricallyConnected (IdentityComponent
203	% G).hom]` queries while the body relies on a sorry-bodied helper.
204	% Post-demotion, consumers must thread `letI :=
205	% identityComponent_geometricallyConnected G` explicitly. Verified by
206	% `lean_verify` that `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` is now
207	% kernel-only.
208	\begin{lemma}
209	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
210	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
212	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
213	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
214	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
216	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
217	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
218	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
219	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
220	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
221	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
222	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
223	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
224	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
225	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
226	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
227	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
228	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
229	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
230	  connected.
231	\end{lemma}
232	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
233	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
234	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
235	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
236	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
237	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
238	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
239	% ring is a field is connected.''
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
242	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
243	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
244	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
245	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
246	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
247	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
248	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
249	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
250	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
251	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
252	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
253	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
254	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
255	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
256	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
257	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
258	  is therefore connected.
259	
260	  Spelled out: this lemma is the iter-194 first-class formalisation of
261	  the Stacks 037Q iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1) specialised to the
262	  presence of a \(k\)-rational section (which automatically supplies
263	  the \enquote{algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(K\) equals \(k\)}
264	  condition via the section's pullback on global sections — see
265	  Stacks 037R for that step).
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	% Note: the lemma above is the substrate needed for
269	% thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes via the route described
270	% in the proof block below. iter-194+ prover work closes its body.
271	
272	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
273	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
274	% (3).)
275	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
276	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
277	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
278	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
279	% \(G^0\) is open too.
280	%
281	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
282	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
283	% extending \(k\).
284	%
285	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
286	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
287	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
288	%
289	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
290	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
291	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
292	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
293	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
294	% [\dots]
295	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
296	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
297	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
298	% [\dots]
299	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
300	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
301	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
302	\begin{proof}
303	  We establish
304	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
305	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
306	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
307	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
308	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
309	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
310	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
311	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
312	
313	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
314	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
315	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
316	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
317	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
318	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
319	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
320	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
321	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
322	
323	  \emph{Base-change commutation
324	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
325	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
326	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
327	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
328	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
329	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
330	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
331	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
332	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
333	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
334	
335	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
336	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
337	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
338	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
339	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
340	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
341	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
342	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
343	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
344	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
345	
346	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
347	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
348	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
349	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
350	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
351	  \(\overline k\), the product
352	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
353	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
354	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
355	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
356	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
357	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
358	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
359	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
360	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
361	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
362	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
363	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
364	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
368	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
369	
370	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
371	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
372	
373	\begin{definition}\leanok
374	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
375	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
377	  
378	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
379	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
380	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
381	  % L2921-L2929).
382	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
383	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
384	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
385	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
386	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
388	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
389	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
390	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
391	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
392	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
393	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
394	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
395	  % \(k\).
396	  %  \end{prp}"
397	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
398	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
399	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
400	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
401	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
402	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
403	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
404	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
405	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
406	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
407	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
408	  extension of the base field.
409	\end{definition}
410	
411	\section{The degree map}
412	\label{sec:pic_degree}
413	
414	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
415	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
416	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
417	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
418	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
419	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
420	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
421	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
422	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
423	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
424	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
425	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
426	degree.
427	
428	\begin{definition}\leanok
429	[Degree of a Picard class]
430	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
432	  
433	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
434	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
435	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
436	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
437	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
438	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
439	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
440	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
441	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
442	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
443	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
444	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
445	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
446	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
447	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
448	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
449	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
450	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
451	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
452	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
453	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
454	  \[
455	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
456	  \]
457	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
458	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
459	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
460	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
461	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
462	  \[
463	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
464	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
465	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
466	  \]
467	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
468	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
469	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
470	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
471	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
472	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
473	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
474	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
475	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
476	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
477	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
478	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
479	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
480	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
481	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
482	  of degree \(d\).
483	\end{definition}
484	
485	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
486	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
487	
488	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
489	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
490	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
491	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
492	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
493	automatically.
494	
495	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
496	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
497	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
498	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
499	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
500	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
501	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
502	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
503	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
504	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
505	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
506	% block.
507	
508	\begin{theorem}\leanok
509	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
510	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
512	
513	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
514	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
515	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
516	  % L3990-L3996).
517	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
518	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
519	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
520	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
521	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
522	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
523	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
524	  % \end{ex}"
525	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
526	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
527	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
528	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
529	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
530	  % Exercise~REF."
531	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
532	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
533	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
535	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
536	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
537	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
538	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
539	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
540	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
541	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
542	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
543	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
544	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
545	  separately stated.)
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	\leanok
551	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
552	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
554	
555	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
556	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
557	  % PDF p.~92).
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
559	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
560	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
561	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
562	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
563	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
564	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
565	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
566	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
567	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
568	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
569	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
570	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
571	  \[
572	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
573	  \]
574	\end{theorem}
575	
576	\begin{theorem}
577	
578	\leanok
579	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
580	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
582	
583	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
584	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
585	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
586	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
587	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
588	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
589	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
590	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
591	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
592	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
593	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
594	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
595	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
596	  if and only if the degree
597	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
598	  vanishes. Equivalently,
599	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
600	\end{theorem}
601	
602	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
603	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
604	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
605	% identity-component substrate.)
606	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
607	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
608	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
609	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
610	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
611	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
612	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
613	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
614	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
615	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
616	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
617	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
618	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
619	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
620	% everywhere."
621	\begin{proof}
622	  We establish
623	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
624	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
625	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
626	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
627	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
628	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
629	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
630	
631	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
632	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
633	  conjuncts from four inputs.
634	
635	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
636	  By
637	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
638	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
639	  type by
640	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
641	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
642	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
643	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
644	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
645	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
646	
647	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
648	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
649	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
650	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
651	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
652	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
653	
654	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
655	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
656	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
657	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
658	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
659	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
660	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
661	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
662	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
663	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
664	
665	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
666	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
667	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
668	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
669	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
670	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
671	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
672	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
673	
674	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
675	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
676	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
677	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
678	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
679	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
680	
681	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
682	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
683	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
684	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
685	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
686	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
687	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
688	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
689	  the open identity component, and
690	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
691	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
692	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
693	
694	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
695	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
696	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
697	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
698	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
699	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
700	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
701	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
702	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
703	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
704	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
705	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
706	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
707	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
708	  zero on \(C\)'').
709	\end{proof}
710

712	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
713	
714	The Lean target file is
715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
716	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
717	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
718	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
719	
720	\begin{itemize}

722	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
723	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
724	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
725	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,

734	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
