781	    underlying scheme).
782	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
783	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
784	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
785	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
786	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
787	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
788	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
789	\end{itemize}
790	
791	The abstract identity-component substrate
792	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
793	conclusion-specific theorems
794	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
795	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
796	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
797	\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
798	is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
799	follow-up iteration. The downstream
800	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
801	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
802	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
803	Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
804	live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
805	smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.
806	
807	\section{Out of scope}
808	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
809	
810	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
811	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
812	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
813	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
814	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
815	
816	\begin{itemize}
817	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
818	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
819	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
820	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
821	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
822	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
823	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
824	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
825	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
826	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
827	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
828	    Route~A.
829	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
830	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
831	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
832	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
833	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
834	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
835	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}
87

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}
195	
196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}
479	
480	\section{Lean encoding}
481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}
523	
524	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
525	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
526	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
527	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
528	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).
529

3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Pic0AbelianVariety.lean
4

14	% formation commuting with base change) and adds the curve-specific
15	% input: the tangent-space isomorphism
16	% \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), the resulting

18	% translation), properness inherited from the projectivity of
19	% \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) for \(C/k\) geometrically normal, and the assembly

21	%
22	% NOTE (planner directive iter-192). The Lean target file

26	
27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
41	
42	\begin{theorem}\leanok
43	[Tangent space at the identity: \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]
44	  \label{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}
45	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso}
46	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
47	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
48	        def:genus}
49	
50	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~thm:tgtsp;
51	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~41 (read from
52	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3265-L3270).
53	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
54	  % "\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
55	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
56	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Let \(\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\) denote the
57	  % tangent space at \(0\).  Then
58	  %    \(\)\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
59	  %  \end{thm}"
60	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
61	  Thm.~thm:tgtsp; cf.\ [Hartshorne], ``Algebraic Geometry'', GTM 52,
62	  IV.1.1 (genus def.\ \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)).}
63	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
64	  \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
65	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the
66	  Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of
67	  \(k\)-vector spaces
68	  \[
69	    \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \;\cong\; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C).
70	  \]
71	  In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals
72	  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
73	  (\cref{def:genus}).
74	\end{theorem}
75	
76	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
77	% L3358-L3367 + L3404-L3407: the construction of the comparison map and
78	% its identification as an isomorphism. Kleiman's full proof spans
79	% L3271-L3408 and is too long for one verbatim block, so we reproduce
80	% the two paragraphs that contain the load-bearing exact sequence and
81	% the conclusion; see Notes for Plan Agent.)
82	% "To compute this kernel, set \(X_\ve:=X\ox_kk_\ve\), and form the truncated
83	% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
84	%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)
85	%  where the first map takes a local section \(b\) to \(1+b\ve\).  This
86	% sequence is split by the map \(\mc O_{\!X}^*\to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*\) defined by
87	% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
88	% sequence of Abelian groups:
89	%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
90	%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
91	% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
92	% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
93	% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
94	% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
95	\begin{proof}
96	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
97	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
98	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
99	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
100	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
101	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
102	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
103	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
104	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
105	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
106	
107	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
108	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
109	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
110	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
111	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
112	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
113	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
114	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
115	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
117	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
118	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
119	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
120	  \[
121	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
122	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
123	  \]
124	
125	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
126	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
127	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
128	  abelian groups
129	  \[
130	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
131	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
132	  \]
133	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
134	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
135	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
136	  \[
137	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
138	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
139	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
140	  \]
141	
142	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
143	  represents the étale-sheafification of
144	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
145	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
146	  \[
147	    \begin{array}{ccc}
148	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
149	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
150	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
151	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
152	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
153	    \end{array}
154	  \]
155	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
156	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
157	  invocation legitimised by
158	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
159	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
160	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
161	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
162	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
163	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
164	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
165	
166	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
167	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
168	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
169	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
170	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
171	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
172	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
173	
174	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
175	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
176	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
177	\end{proof}
178	
179	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
180	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
181	
182	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
183	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
184	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
185	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
186	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
187	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
188	dimension \(g\).
189	
190	\begin{theorem}\leanok
191	[Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
192	  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth}
194	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
195	        def:pic_zero_subscheme,
196	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
197	
198	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0;
199	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~44 (read from
200	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3427 +
201	  % L3442-L3446).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
204	  % \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
205	  %    \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
206	  %  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
207	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
208	  % everywhere.
209	  %  \end{cor}"
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:ch0}
212	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
213	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), then
214	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere.
215	  %  \end{cor}"
216	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
217	  Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties''
218	  (course notes, 2008), \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (dimension of \(J\)
219	  equals the genus).}
220	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
221	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
222	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension
223	  \(g\): the structural morphism
224	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is smooth, and every closed point of
225	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.
226	\end{theorem}
227	
228	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
229	% L3428-L3439: the proof of Cor.~cor:sm. We reproduce the full proof,
230	% which deduces smoothness everywhere from smoothness at \(0\) by
231	% translation.)
232	% "\begin{proof} Plainly we may assume \(k\) is algebraically closed. Then,
233	% given any closed point \(\lambda\in\IPic_{X/k}\), there is an automorphism
234	% of \(\IPic_{X/k}\) that carries 0 to \(\lambda\), namely, ``multiplication''
235	% by \(\lambda\).  So \(\IPic_{X/k}\) has the same dimension at \(\lambda\) as
236	% at 0, and \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is
237	% smooth at 0.
238	%
239	% By general principles, \(\dim_0 \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\),
240	% and equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\).
241	% Moreover, \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\) if and only if it is smooth
242	% at \(0\) since \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, the corollary
243	% results from Theorem~\ref{thm:tgtsp}.
244	%  \end{proof}"
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
247	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
248	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
249	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
250	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
251	
252	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
253	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
254	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
255	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
256	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
257	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
258	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
259	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
260	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
261	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
262	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
263	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
264	  \(0\).
265	
266	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
267	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
268	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
269	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
270	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
271	  smoothness. Hence
272	  \(
273	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
274	  \)
275	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
276	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
277	
278	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
279	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
280	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
281	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
282	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
283	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
284	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
285	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
286	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
287	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
288	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
289	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
290	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
291	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
292	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
293	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
294	
295	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
296	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
297	\end{proof}
298	
299	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
300	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
301	
302	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
303	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
304	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
305	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
306	
307	\begin{theorem}\leanok
308	[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
309	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
311	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
312	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
313	
314	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
315	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
316	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
317	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
318	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
319	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
320	  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
321	  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
322	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.
323	  %  \end{thm}"
324	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
325	  Thm.~th:qpp\&p; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes,
326	  2008), \S I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p.~27).}
327	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
328	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
329	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
330	  particular proper: the structural morphism
331	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence
332	  satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of
333	  properness.
334	\end{theorem}
335	
336	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
337	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
338	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
339	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
340	% geometric normality of \(C\).)
341	% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
342	% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
343	% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
344	% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
345	% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
346	% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
347	%
348	% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
349	% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
350	% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
351	% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
352	% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
353	% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
354	% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
355	% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
356	% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
357	% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
358	% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
359	% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
360	\begin{proof}
361	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
362	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
363	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
364	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
365	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
366	
367	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
368	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
369	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
370	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
371	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
372	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
373	  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
374	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
375	  is projective and geometrically integral.
376	
377	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
378	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
379	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
380	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
381	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
382	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
383	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
384	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
385	
386	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
387	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
388	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
389	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
390	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
391	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
392	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
393	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
394	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
395	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
396	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
397	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
398	  is constant.
399	
400	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
401	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
402	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
403	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
404	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
405	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
406	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
407	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
408	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
409	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
410	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
411	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
412	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
413	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
414	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
415	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
416	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
417	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
418	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
419	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
420	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
421	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
422	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
423	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
424	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
425	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
426	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
427	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
428	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
429	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
430	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
431	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
432	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
433	  properness.
434	\end{proof}

436	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
437	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}
438	
439	Geometric irreducibility is delivered directly by the abstract
440	identity-component substrate
441	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
442	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
443	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.

454	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).

468	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
469	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
470	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
471	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme

477	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
478	        thm:fga_pic_representability}

480	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
481	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By

483	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite

491	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
492	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
493	
494	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
495	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
496	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
497	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
498	identity-component group-scheme structure of
499	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
500	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
501	gate for Route~A.
502	
503	\begin{theorem}\leanok
504	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
505	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
507	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
508	        thm:pic0_proper,
509	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
510	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
511	
512	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
513	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
514	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
515	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
516	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
517	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
518	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
519	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
520	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
521	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
522	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
523	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
524	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
525	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
526	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
527	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
528	  % Exercise~REF."
529	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
530	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
531	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac (the Jacobian
532	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
533	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
535	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
536	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
537	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
538	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
539	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
540	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
541	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
542	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
543	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
544	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
545	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{proof}
549	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
550	        thm:pic0_proper,
551	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
552	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
553	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
554	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
555	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
556	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
557	
558	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
559	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
560	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
561	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
562	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
564	
565	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
566	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
567	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
568	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
569	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
570	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
571	
572	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
573	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
574	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
575	
576	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
577	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
578	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
579	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
580	
581	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
582	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
583	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
584	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
585	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
586	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
587	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
588	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
589	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
590	
591	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac: for \(X/S\)
592	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
593	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
594	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
595	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
596	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
597	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
598	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
599	\end{proof}

658	\section{Out of scope}
659	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety_out_of_scope}
660	
661	This chapter packages \emph{only} the five A.3.iii--vii steps. The
662	following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters
663	and are explicitly out of scope here:
664	
665	\begin{itemize}
666	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
667	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
668	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
669	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}.
670	  \item \textbf{Autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
671	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6; Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac, Abel map) ---
672	    not needed for the Jacobian's existence as an abelian variety,
673	    hence out of scope.
674	  \item \textbf{Torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
675	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
676	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
677	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
678	    Route~A.
679	  \item \textbf{Genus-zero degenerate case.} When \(g(C) = 0\),
680	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the trivial group scheme \(\Spec k\)
681	    (Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)); this is consistent with the
682	    abelian-variety assembly (a point is trivially an abelian variety
683	    of dimension \(0\)) and is handled uniformly here, but is not
684	    separately stated.
685	\end{itemize}

14	% through the Abel map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), which factors
15	% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
16	% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%

21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
26	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
27	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}

110	\section{The Quot functor}
111	\label{sec:quot_functor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}\leanok
114	[Quot functor]
115	  \label{def:quot_functor}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
117	  
118	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
119	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
120	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
121	  % + L481-L498).
122	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
123	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
124	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
125	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
126	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
127	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
128	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
129	  %
130	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
131	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
132	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
133	  %
134	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
135	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
136	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
137	  %
138	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
139	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
140	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
141	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
142	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
143	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
144	  %  well-defined, which gives
145	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
146	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
147	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
148	  %  [...]
149	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
150	  %  as a co-product
151	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
152	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
153	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
154	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
155	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
156	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
157	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
158	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
159	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
160	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
161	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
162	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
163	  \[
164	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
165	  \]
166	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
167	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
170	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
171	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
172	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
173	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
174	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
175	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
176	      \(\Phi\).
177	  \end{enumerate}
178	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
179	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
180	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
181	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
182	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
183	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
184	\end{definition}
185	
186	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
187	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
188	
189	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
190	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
191	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
192	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
193	
194	\begin{definition}\leanok
195	[Grassmannian scheme]
196	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
198	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
199	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
200	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
201	  % + L773-L850).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
204	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
205	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
206	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
207	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
208	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
209	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
210	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
211	  %  contravariant functor
212	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
213	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
214	  %  set of all equivalence classes
215	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
216	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
217	  %  [...]
218	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
219	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
220	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
221	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
222	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
223	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
224	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
225	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
226	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
227	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
228	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
229	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
230	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
231	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
232	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
233	  \[
234	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
235	    \qquad
236	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
237	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
238	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
239	  \]
240	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely $\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
241	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\( (the Quot functor for \)X = S\(, \)E = V$,
242	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	\begin{theorem}\leanok
246	[Representability of the Grassmannian]
247	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
248	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
249	  
250	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
251	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
252	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
253	  % L798-L930).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
255	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
256	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
257	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
258	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
259	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
260	  %  the schemes
261	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
262	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
263	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
264	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
265	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
266	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
267	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
268	  %  [...]
269	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
270	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
271	  %  [...]
272	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
273	  %  the determinant line bundle
274	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
275	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
276	  %  [...]
277	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
278	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
279	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
280	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
281	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
282	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
283	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
284	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
285	  Explained Ch.~5).}
286	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
287	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
288	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
289	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
290	  equipped with a tautological quotient
291	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
292	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
293	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
294	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
295	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)$ -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
296	\end{theorem}
297	
298	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
299	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
300	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
301	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
302	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
303	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
304	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
305	\begin{proof}
306	  
307	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
308	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
309	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
310	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
311	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
312	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
313	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
314	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
315	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
316	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
317	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
318	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
319	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
320	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
321	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
322	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
323	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
324	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
325	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
326	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
327	
328	  The universal quotient $u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
329	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\( is given on \)U^I\( by the matrix \)X^I$
330	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
331	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
332	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
333	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
334	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
335	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
336	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
337	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
338	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
339	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
340	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
341	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
342	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism $T \to U^I \subset
343	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\(. Base change from \)\mathbb{Z}$ to a general noetherian
344	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
345	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
346	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
347	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
348	\end{proof}
