350	\section{Representability of the Quot scheme}
351	\label{sec:quot_representable}
352	
353	\begin{theorem}\leanok
354	[Representability of the Quot functor]
355	  \label{thm:quot_representable}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Main Existence Theorems"
359	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
360	  % L2220-L2273); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.
361	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
362	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi: X\to S\) a projective morphism,
363	  %  and \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\). Then for any
364	  %  coherent \({\mathcal O}_X\)-module \(E\) and any polynomial \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\),
365	  %  the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) is representable
366	  %  by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem
368	  (Altman--Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.}
369	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a projective morphism,
370	  \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent
371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Then the
372	  functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) of \cref{def:quot_functor} is
373	  representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\),
374	  equipped with a universal quotient
375	  \[
376	    q^{\mathrm{univ}} :
377	    \pi^*_{\Quot} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}}
378	    \quad \text{on } X \times_S \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S},
379	  \]
380	  flat over \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) with constant Hilbert polynomial
381	  \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case
382	  \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\): \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} = \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)
383	  represents the functor of \(T\)-flat closed subschemes
384	  \(Y \subset X_T\) with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\).
385	\end{theorem}
386	
387	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]
388	% \S 5). Each sub-step is recorded with its own SOURCE QUOTE in the
389	% sub-lemmas below. The structural skeleton of the proof is restated in
390	% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.
391	\begin{proof}
392	  
393	  The proof has four steps; each step is a sub-lemma in the next
394	  sub-sections.
395	  \begin{enumerate}
396	    \item \textbf{(Boundedness, \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}.)} Pick the
397	      Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound $m = m(\mathrm{rank}\,V,
398	      \mathrm{rank}\,W, \Phi)$ uniformly across all fibers (by Mumford's
399	      \(m\)-regularity theorem applied to \(E_s\) and to all coherent
400	      quotients of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\)). For \(r \ge m\)
401	      and any \(T \to S\) and any \(T\)-flat quotient
402	      \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
403	      \(\Phi\), the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\)
404	      and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\) and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\)
405	      vanish for \(i \ge 1\) (where \(\mathcal{G} = \ker q\)), and the
406	      direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
407	      \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of ranks fixed by the
408	      data; the canonical maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\)
409	      are surjective. Cohomology-and-base-change (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology},
410	      Stacks tag 02KH) propagates these properties across \(T\)-base change.
411	    \item \textbf{(Grassmannian embedding, \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}.)}
412	      Reduce by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} to the case
413	      \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V, W\) locally free
414	      on \(S\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\). Fix \(r \ge m\).
415	      The Grassmannian embedding
416	      \[
417	        \alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
418	        \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
419	          W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
420	        \right)
421	      \]
422	      sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) to the locally free
423	      quotient \((\pi_T)_*(q(r)) : (\pi_T)_* E_T(r) \to (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\).
424	      This is injective on \(T\)-valued points: from the map $T \to
425	      \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$ one recovers
426	      the kernel-pull-back \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r) \to E_T(r)\),
427	      whose cokernel is \(q(r) : E_T(r) \to \mathcal{F}(r)\);
428	      twisting back by \(-r\) recovers \(q\).
429	    \item \textbf{(Locally closed in the Grassmannian via flattening
430	      stratification.)} Let $T_0 = \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V,
431	      \Phi(r))$ with universal quotient
432	      \(f : (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_{T_0} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{J}\)
433	      and kernel \(\mathcal{K}\). Form the composite
434	      $h : \pi_{T_0}^* \mathcal{K} \hookrightarrow
435	      \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V) = \pi_{T_0}^* (\pi_{T_0})_* E_{T_0}
436	      \twoheadrightarrow E_{T_0}$
437	      and let \(q := \mathrm{coker}(h) : E_{T_0} \to \mathcal{F}\). By the
438	      flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
439	      of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\)
440	      on \(X_{T_0}\), there exists a locally closed subscheme
441	      \(T_0^\Phi \subseteq T_0\) such that for any \(\phi : Y \to T_0\), the
442	      pulled-back cokernel \(\mathcal{F}_Y\) is \(Y\)-flat with fiberwise
443	      Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) iff \(\phi\) factors through \(T_0^\Phi\).
444	      The pair \((T_0^\Phi, \text{universal cokernel})\) represents
445	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\).
446	    \item \textbf{(Closed, hence projective,
447	      \cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}.)} The functor
448	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion of
449	      properness with respect to DVRs (Exercise (7) of \S 1, proved
450	      using that flatness over a DVR is torsion-freeness). Combined
451	      with the locally closed embedding of step 3 into the projective
452	      $\mathrm{Gr}_S \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^{\Phi(r)}
453	      (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V))$, this upgrades to a \emph{closed}
454	      embedding. Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a projective \(S\)-scheme.
455	  \end{enumerate}
456	  Finally, by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}, the general case
457	  (arbitrary \(X\) projective over \(S\) and arbitrary coherent \(E\)) follows
458	  from the case \(X = \mathbb{P}(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\): pick a closed
459	  embedding \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) and write \(E\) as a coherent quotient
460	  of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)\) for some vector bundle \(W\) on \(S\) and integer \(\nu\);
461	  the Quot scheme of the original data is then a closed subscheme of the
462	  Quot scheme of the universal data, by the closed-embedding clause of
463	  \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}.
464	\end{proof}
465	
466	\section{Sub-lemmas of the construction}
467	\label{sec:quot_sub_lemmas}
468	
469	\begin{lemma}[Reduction to the universal case]
470	  \label{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}
471	  
472	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Reduction to the case of
473	  % \(\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}\)"
474	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
475	  % L2294-L2332).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "It is enough to prove Theorem REF in the special case
478	  %  that \(X = \PP(V)\) and
479	  %  \(E= \pi^*(W)\) where \(V\) and \(W\) are vector bundles on \(S\), as
480	  %  a consequence of the next lemma.
481	  %
482	  %  \begin{lemma}
483	  %  (i) Let \(\nu\) be any integer.
484	  %  Then tensoring by \(L^{\nu}\) gives an isomorphism of
485	  %  functors from \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) to
486	  %  \(\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}\) where the polynomial
487	  %  \(\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]\) is defined by \(\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)\).
488	  %
489	  %  (ii) Let \(\phi : E \to G\) be a surjective homomorphism of
490	  %  coherent sheaves on \(X\). Then the corresponding natural transformation
491	  %  \(\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)
492	  %  is a closed embedding.
493	  %  \end{lemma}"
494	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
495	  Let \(S\), \(X\), \(L\), \(E\), \(\Phi\) be as in \cref{def:quot_functor}.
496	  \begin{enumerate}
497	    \item (Twist-shift.) For \(\nu \in \mathbb{Z}\), tensoring by
498	      \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) gives an isomorphism of functors
499	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \xrightarrow{\sim}
500	      \Quot^{\Psi,L}_{E(\nu)/X/S}\( with \)\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
501	    \item (Surjection induces closed embedding.) Given a surjection
502	      \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) of coherent sheaves on \(X\), the
503	      induced natural transformation
504	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \to \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a closed
505	      embedding of functors.
506	  \end{enumerate}
507	  Consequently, if \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\pi^* W / \mathbb{P}(V)/S}\) is
508	  representable for all \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on \(S\), then so is
509	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) for every \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) closed and
510	  every coherent \(E\) on \(X\) that is a quotient of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X\) for
511	  some \(W\) and \(\nu\).
512	\end{lemma}
513	
514	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just
515	% says that given any locally noetherian scheme \(T\) and a family
516	% \(\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)\), there exists
517	% a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) with the following universal property:
518	% for any locally noetherian scheme \(U\) and a morphism \(f: U\to T\),
519	% the pulled back homomorphism of \({\mathcal O}_{X_U}\)-modules \(q_U : E_U \to \F_U\)
520	% factors via the pulled back homomorphism \(\phi_U : E_U \to G_U\)
521	% if and only if \(U \to T\) factors via \(T'\hra T\).
522	% This is satisfied by taking \(T'\) to be the vanishing scheme
523	% for the composite homomorphism \(\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F\)
524	% of coherent sheaves on \(X_T\) (see Remark REF),
525	% which makes sense here as both \(\ker(\phi)\) and \(\F\) are coherent
526	% on \(X_T\) and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\)."
527	\begin{proof}
528	  
529	  (i) is direct from the definitions: tensoring with \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) is
530	  an autoequivalence of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (and on every \(X_T\)),
531	  preserving surjectivity, \(T\)-flatness, and properness of support, and
532	  shifting the Hilbert polynomial by \(\nu\) in the variable.
533	
534	  (ii) Given a family \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) in
535	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), the locus where \(q\) factors through
536	  \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) is cut out by the vanishing of the
537	  composite $\ker(\phi)|_{X_T} \hookrightarrow E_T \xrightarrow{q}
538	  \mathcal{F}\(. Because \)\mathcal{F}\( is \)T$-flat, the schematic image of
539	  this composite descends to a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) such that
540	  \(f : U \to T\) factors through \(T'\) iff \(q_U\) factors through \(\phi_U\).
541	  Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \hookrightarrow \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\)
542	  is a closed embedding of subfunctors.
543	\end{proof}
544	
545	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
546	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
549	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
550	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
551	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
552	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
554	  % "We consider the sheaf \(E = \pi^*(W)\) on \(X=\PP(V)\) where
555	  %  \(V\) is a vector bundle on \(S\),
556	  %  and take \(L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)\).
557	  %  For any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point
558	  %  \(s\) of \(S\), we have an isomorphism
559	  %  \(\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k\) where \(n=\rank(V)-1\),
560	  %  and the restricted sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is isomorphic to
561	  %  \(\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}\) where \(p=\rank(W)\).
562	  %  It follows from Theorem REF
563	  %  that given any \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\), there exists an integer
564	  %  \(m\) which depends only on \(\rank(V)\), \(\rank(W)\) and \(\Phi\),
565	  %  such that for any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point \(s\) of \(S\),
566	  %  the sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is \(m\)-regular, and for any
567	  %  coherent quotient \(q: E_s \to \F\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) with
568	  %  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\), the sheaf \(\F\) and the kernel sheaf
569	  %  \(\G\subset E_s\) of \(q\) are both \(m\)-regular."
570	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity" (FGA Explained
571	  Ch.~5).}
572	  Let \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on
573	  \(S\) of ranks \(n+1\), \(p\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\), and
574	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). There exists an integer
575	  \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\), depending only on \(n\), \(p\), and \(\Phi\), such that
576	  for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) and every coherent quotient
577	  \(q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
578	  \(\Phi\), the sheaves \(E_s\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\ker q\) on \(X_s\) are all
579	  \(m\)-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
580	  every \(T \to S\) and every \(T\)-flat quotient
581	  \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) on \(X_T\) with Hilbert
582	  polynomial \(\Phi\) and every \(r \ge m\), the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
583	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
584	  hold: the direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
585	  \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of fixed ranks; the
586	  canonical surjections \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)\)
587	  hold; and the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)\) vanish for
588	  \(i \ge 1\).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
592	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
593	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
594	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
595	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
596	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across \(T\).
597	\begin{proof}
598	  
599	  Each geometric fiber \(E_s\) is a trivial bundle on \(\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}\)
600	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
601	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
602	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
603	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
604	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
605	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
606	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
607	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the
608	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
609	  tag 02KH) along \(T \to S\): cohomology of flat families commutes with
610	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
611	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
612	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
613	  surjectivity.
614	\end{proof}
615	
616	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) is injective]
617	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
618	  
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
620	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
621	  % L2409-L2451).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "We now fix a positive integer \(r\) such that \(r\ge m\).
624	  %  Note that the rank of \({\pi_T}_*\F(r)\) is \(\Phi(r)\)
625	  %  and \(\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V\).
626	  %  Therefore the surjective homomorphism
627	  %  \({\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
628	  %  defines an element of the set
629	  %  \(\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)\).
630	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
631	  %  \(\)\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
632	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))\(\)
633	  %  It associates to \(q: E_T \to \F\) the quotient
634	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\).
635	  %
636	  %  The above morphism \(\alpha\) is injective because the quotient
637	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) can be recovered from
638	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
639	  %  as follows."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
641	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
642	  In the universal setup \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\), fix
643	  \(r \ge m\) from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \alpha :
646	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
647	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
648	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
649	    \right),
650	    \qquad
651	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
652	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
653	  \]
654	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank \(\Phi(r)\), and the
655	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
656	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V\( gives the source) and \emph{injective} on \)T$-points:
657	  the data of the locally free quotient at level \(r\) determines
658	  \(q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}\).
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  Suppose \(\alpha(T)\) sends both \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle\) and
664	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\) to the same \(T\)-point of the
665	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
666	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
667	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
668	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
669	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
670	  \[
671	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
672	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
673	  \]
674	  so \(q_i(r)\) is recovered as the cokernel of \(h\); twisting by \(-r\)
675	  recovers \(q_i\), giving \(q_1 = q_2\) and hence
676	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\).
677	\end{proof}
678	
679	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for \(\Quot\)]
680	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
681	  
682	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
683	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
684	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "The functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) satisfies the following valuative
687	  %  criterion for properness over \(S\): given any discrete valuation ring
688	  %  \(R\) over \(S\) with quotient field \(K\), the restriction map
689	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\(\)
690	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
691	  %  Given any coherent quotient
692	  %  \(q: E_K \to \F\) on \(X_R\) which defines an element \(\langle \F, q\rangle\)
693	  %  of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\).
694	  %  Let \(\ov{\F}\) be the image of the composite homomorphism
695	  %  \(E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F\) where \(j : X_K \hra X_R\) is the open
696	  %  inclusion. Let \(\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}\) be the induced surjection.
697	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
698	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) is an element of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)\)
699	  %  which maps to \(\langle \F, q\rangle\), and is the unique such element.
700	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
701	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
703	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
704	  The functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion
705	  for properness over \(S\): for every discrete valuation ring \(R\) with
706	  fraction field \(K\) and every morphism \(\Spec R \to S\), the restriction
707	  map
708	  \[
709	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
710	    \longrightarrow
711	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
712	  \]
713	  is bijective.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  
718	  Given a \(\Spec K\)-point \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) on \(X_K\),
719	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
720	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
721	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
722	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
723	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
724	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR
725	  \(=\) torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
726	  \(\mathcal{F}_K\). The pair \((\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})\) is
727	  the unique extension of \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) to a
728	  \(\Spec R\)-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
729	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
730	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
731	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.
732	\end{proof}

734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}

791	\subsection{Project-side base-change substrate}
792	\label{subsec:quot_project_basechange}
793	
794	The Lean encoding of \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} (and the
795	Quot construction's iso between pushforward and tensor-pullback for the
796	universal section pattern) relies on four project-side declarations
797	introduced in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. These
798	are typed-sorry today: each carries a substantive Lean type that
799	encodes the intended mathematical statement, with the body deferred to
800	later iters (see \cref{sec:quot_lean_encoding} for the
801	phase-by-phase plan).
802	
803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}
819	
820	\begin{theorem}\leanok
821	[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines]
822	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso}
824	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
825	  For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open
826	  \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component
827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.
832	\end{theorem}
833	
834	\begin{theorem}\leanok
835	[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism]
836	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_isIso}
837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso}
838	  \uses{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
839	  Globalising the stalk-wise statement
840	  \cref{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}: the natural
841	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of
842	  \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism of

847	\begin{definition}\leanok
848	[Tensor-presentation of the pullback-of-sections functor]
849	  \label{def:quot_pullback_app_isoTensor}
850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_app_isoTensor}
851	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
852	  Given a morphism \(g : X \to Y\) of schemes, affine opens
853	  \(U \subseteq Y\), \(V \subseteq X\) with \(g(V) \subseteq U\), and a
854	  quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{N}\) on \(Y\), the
855	  \emph{tensor-presentation iso} packages the canonical iso
856	  \(\Gamma(V,\, g^* \mathcal{N}) \;\cong\;
857	   \Gamma(V,\, \mathcal{O}_X) \otimes_{\Gamma(U,\, \mathcal{O}_Y)}
858	     \Gamma(U,\, \mathcal{N})\)
859	  as a `LinearEquiv`. This is the project-side rfl-bridge used by the
860	  consumer \(\text{Quot}\)-functor iso assembly: it discharges the
861	  pullback-app step of the Tilde-isoTop route (Stacks tag 01HQ / 01I8)
862	  to a base-change-of-scalars identity. The body factors as
863	  (Step 1, axiom-clean): the underlying linear map
864	  \texttt{pullback\_app\_isoTensor\_unitAtV};
865	  (Step 2, project-side typed sorry): the
866	  \texttt{IsBaseChange}-packaging step
867	  \texttt{pullback\_app\_isoTensor\_isBaseChange} reducing to
868	  Stacks 02KE bijectivity of the canonical map.

871	\begin{definition}
872	
873	\leanok
874	[\(\Sigma\)-pair packaging: section-level LinearEquiv + intertwining]
875	  \label{def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma}
876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv}
877	  \uses{def:quot_pullback_app_isoTensor, def:quot_canonical_basechange_map,
878	        lem:pullback_tildeIso, lem:tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen,
879	        lem:pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restrict}
880	  Iter-188 Lane F-introduced \(\Sigma\)-pair packaging declaration that
881	  bundles BOTH the section-level LinearEquiv
882	  \(\Gamma(V,(g^*\mathcal N)) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\; \Gamma(Y,U) \otimes
883	    \Gamma(N,V)\) AND its intertwining property with the canonical
884	  base-change map into a single \(\Sigma\)-struct returned via

887	  \[
888	    \exists\, (f : \Gamma(V,(\text{pullback}\, g).\mathrm{obj}\,
889	                     \mathcal N) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\;
890	                     \Gamma(X,V) \otimes_{\Gamma(Y,U)} \Gamma(N,V)),\;
891	      \forall x,\; f(1\otimes x)\;=\;\text{baseMap}\,g\,\mathcal N\,e\,x.
892	  \]
893	  This packaging idiom keeps consumer signatures clean and is the project-
894	  side bridge into Mathlib's \texttt{IsBaseChange.of\_equiv} consumer.
895	  Body is gated on \cref{lem:pullback_tildeIso} (Stacks 01HQ) plus a
896	  6-stage Beck-Chevalley intertwining decomposition recorded in
897	  \cref{sec:quot_iter195_bc_substrate} and mirroring the in-file
898	  comment block at
899	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:999-1037}:
900	  \begin{itemize}

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\). The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup
26	% because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, \(\Pic_X\) is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below \(\Pic_X\) is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
191	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp at the pinned Mathlib commit
192	% b80f227 carries a constant-functor placeholder body
193	% ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const _).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of PUnit)),
194	% i.e.\ the constant functor at the trivial abelian group. This is NOT
195	% the relative Picard presheaf
196	% \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) that the definition
197	% above describes; the placeholder merely supplies a type-checking
198	% object of the correct kind. The closure is gated on the upstream
199	% Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap (no
200	% tensor-product \texttt{AddCommGroup} structure on
201	% \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} is available, hence no quotient group
202	% can be built on the Lean side until that gap is closed). DO NOT
203	% promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic \texttt{sync\_leanok}
204	% phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in review, until the body is
205	% replaced with the mathematically correct construction.
206	
207	\begin{lemma}\leanok
208	[Naturality respects the group structure]
209	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
213	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
214	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
215	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
216	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
217	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
220	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
221	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
222	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
223	  %  [\dots]
224	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
225	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
226	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
231	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
232	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
233	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
234	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
235	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
236	  homomorphisms.
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
241	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
242	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
243	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
244	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial at b80f227 carries a
245	% zero AddMonoidHom body (\(0 : \dots \to \dots\)), i.e.\ the zero map
246	% between two trivial-group endpoints, NOT the pullback-descended
247	% homomorphism this lemma describes. Moreover, because the codomain's
248	% \texttt{Zero} instance is derived from the sorry-body
249	% \texttt{addCommGroup} (the file-local genuine sorry of the chapter),
250	% the closure also inherits \texttt{sorryAx} via that typeclass leak;
251	% it is therefore not even axiom-clean. The closure is gated on the
252	% upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO
253	% NOT promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic
254	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in
255	% review, until the body is replaced with the mathematically correct
256	% map descended from \(g_C^*\).
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
265	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
266	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
267	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
268	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
269	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
270	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
271	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
272	\end{proof}

274	\begin{theorem}\leanok
275	[\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]
276	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
278	  
279	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
280	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
281	  % abelian groups)
282	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
283	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
284	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
285	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
286	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
287	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
288	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
289	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
290	  The assignment \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
291	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of
292	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
293	  \[
294	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
295	                                     \AddCommGroup
296	  \]
297	  on the category of \(k\)-schemes.
298	\end{theorem}

301	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
302	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
303	% above; no external proof is being translated.
304	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
305	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf at b80f227 is defined as
306	% \texttt{PicSharp \_C}, i.e.\ it re-exports the constant-PUnit-functor
307	% placeholder from \texttt{PicSharp} above; it is NOT the canonical
308	% presheaf assembled from the pullback-descended functorial action

311	% monoidal-structure gap. DO NOT promote this block to \leanok via the

313	% formalised in review, until the body is replaced with the
314	% mathematically correct presheaf assembly.

316	
317	  The object map is well defined by
318	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
319	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
320	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by
321	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
322	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
339	
340	\begin{definition}\leanok
341	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
342	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
344	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
345	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
346	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
347	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
348	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
349	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
350	  % below is also corrected.
351	  
352	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
353	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
354	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
355	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
356	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
359	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
360	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)

362	  % topologies  by

365	  %  \end{dfn}

369	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
370	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
371	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
372	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the

374	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
375	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
376	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
377	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
378	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
379	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
380	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
381	  \[
382	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;

386	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)

390	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
391	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
392	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
393	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
394	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
395	\end{definition}

499	
500	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
501	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
502	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
503	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the

509	  below; see its proof sketch for the canonical formulation.

512	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
513	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
514	
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
516	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
517	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
518	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
519	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
520	  % property)
521	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
523	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
524	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
525	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
526	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
527	  % topologies  by
528	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
529	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
530	  %  \end{dfn}
531	  %  [\dots]
532	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
533	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
534	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
535	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
536	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
537	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
538	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
539	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
540	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
541	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
542	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
543	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
544	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
545	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
546	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
547	  \[
548	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
549	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
550	  \]
551	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
552	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
553	  of presheaves of abelian groups
554	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
555	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
556	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
557	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
558	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
559	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
560	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
561	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
562	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
563	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
564	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
565	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
566	  \(\eta\).
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
570	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
571	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
572	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
573	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
574	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
575	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
576	\begin{proof}
577	
578	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
579	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
580	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
581	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
582	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
583	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
584	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
585	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
586	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
587	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
588	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
589	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
590	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
591	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
592	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
593	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
594	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
595	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
596	\end{proof}

673	    set-valued presheaf, transported back through the
674	    \texttt{ConcreteCategory} instance on \texttt{AddCommGrpCat}.
675	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure}
676	    (\cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) packages the fact
677	    that the \'etale sheafification preserves the abelian-group target.
678	    On the Lean side it is the canonical
679	    \texttt{Functor (Schemeᵒᵖ \(\Rightarrow\) AddCommGrpCat)}
680	    obtained by applying
681	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf
682	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat} to
683	    \texttt{PicSharp.presheaf} and then forgetting from the sheaf
684	    category back to a presheaf-of-abelian-groups via
685	    \texttt{(CategoryTheory.sheafToPresheaf etaleTopology
686	    AddCommGrpCat)}. The proof is structural: it identifies
687	    \texttt{etSheaf} (typed as a sheaf of sets via the concrete
688	    plus-construction
689	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafify}) with the
690	    value of \texttt{presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat
691	    PicSharp.presheaf} under the forgetful functor, using
692	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.sheafification\_obj}
693	    (the equality \(\texttt{J.sheafification D}.\mathrm{obj}\, P =
694	    \texttt{J.sheafify}\, P\)) and the fact that the forgetful functor
695	    \(\texttt{AddCommGrpCat} \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves the
696	    multiequalizer / filtered-colimit data used by the
697	    plus-construction (\texttt{plusPlusSheaf} commutes with
698	    \texttt{forget} on a \texttt{ConcreteCategory} target).

701	\paragraph{Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh).}
702	The residual prover gate inside
703	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} (the file
704	implementing the six declarations above) is \emph{not} on the
705	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier --- that typed sorry was closed
706	in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean} during
707	the iter-188 unpacking, where
708	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} was concretised as the subtype
709	\(\{N : (\texttt{pullback}\ \pi_C\ \pi_T).\mathtt{Modules}\ \mathbin{|}\
710	\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\ N\}\) and
711	\texttt{pullbackAlongProjection} / \texttt{pullback\_pullback\_eq} were
712	filled axiom-cleanly using \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback},
713	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}, and
714	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}.
715	
716	The genuine residual gate is the abelian-group instance on the
717	quotient \texttt{Quotient (preimage\_subgroup πC πT)} (the file-local
718	sorry scaffold near \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L205--L235, which the
719	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup} declaration is
720	pinned to). That instance in turn depends on a tensor-product
721	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure on \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}:
722	forming the abelian-group law on isomorphism classes
723	\([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes L']\) requires a tensor-product operation
724	on the carrier and an inverse for invertibles. Mathlib at the pinned
725	commit \texttt{b80f227} ships
726	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
727	the level of \texttt{PresheafOfModules}), but does \emph{not} expose a
728	monoidal-category structure on the higher-level
729	\texttt{Scheme.Modules} category; this is the gap explicitly flagged
730	in \texttt{LineBundlePullback.lean}, L344--L346, with the comment
731	``Mathlib ships \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} but no
732	monoidal structure on \texttt{Scheme.Modules} directly at
733	\texttt{b80f227}'' --- the \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
734	gap.
735	
736	Modulo that single file-local sorry, the five other prover-gate
737	sorries at \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L287--L482 (the bodies of
738	\texttt{PicSharp},
739	\texttt{PicSharp.functorial},
740	\texttt{PicSharp.presheaf},
741	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf}, and
742	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure}) close axiom-cleanly: each
743	consumes \texttt{addCommGroup} as a typeclass hypothesis and threads
744	the resulting group structure through the standard Mathlib pattern
745	(\texttt{QuotientAddGroup} for the quotient, \texttt{Functor.mk} for
746	the presheaf packaging, \texttt{Quotient.lift} respecting the
747	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure for the functorial action, and
748	\texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}
749	for the \'etale sheafification with its abelian-group target).
750	Once the \texttt{addCommGroup} instance is provided, the file
751	collapses from six sorries to one, and the one remaining sorry is
752	gated only on the Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
753	gap rather than on any project-side mathematics (making it an
754	upstream Mathlib-build-mode task, not an A.1.c mathematical gap).
755	
756	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
757	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
758	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
759	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
760	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the

763	\section{Out of scope}
764	\label{sec:relpic_out_of_scope}
765	
766	The following constructions are downstream of this chapter and live in
767	dedicated sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
768	\begin{itemize}
769	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
770	    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- the
771	    Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman existence theorem is the next step but is
772	    not part of this chapter.

774	    \Pic_{(X/S)\zar} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{(X/S)\et} \hookrightarrow
775	    \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$} (Kleiman \S 2, th:cmp) --- the chain of inclusions
776	    under \(\mathcal{O}_S \cong f_*\mathcal{O}_X\); we use only the
777	    \'etale-sheafified functor here, not the full comparison chain.
778	  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3) --- the open subgroup
779	    scheme cut out by Hilbert polynomials, treated in a downstream chapter.
780	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4) --- consumes the full
781	    Route~A stack.
782	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on the representing object} --- once
783	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is representable (A.2.c), its representing scheme
784	    inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from the abelian-group-valued
785	    target via the Yoneda embedding; that refinement is part of A.2.c, not
786	    of this chapter.
787	\end{itemize}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`

76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

197	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
198	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
199	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
200	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;

203	  there is a natural bijection

207	  \]
208	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
209	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
210	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).

235	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
236	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
237	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
238	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
239	  (Theorem~REF) together with the
240	  base-change identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\)
241	  (Theorem~REF). Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
242	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
243	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
244	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
245	  of Theorem~REF.
246	\end{proof}

494	\section{Lean encoding}
495	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
496	
497	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
498	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
499	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
500	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
501	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
502	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
503	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
504	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
505	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
506	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
507	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
508	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
509	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
510	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
511	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
512	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
513	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
514	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
515	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
516	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
517	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
518
