1065	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
1066	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
1067	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
1068	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
1069	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
1070	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
1071	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
1072	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
1073	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
1074	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
1075	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
1076	  extension degree:
1077	  \[
1078	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
1079	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
1080	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1081	  \]
1082	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
1083	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
1084	\end{lemma}
1085	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
1086	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
1087	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
1088	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
1089	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
1090	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
1091	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
1092	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
1093	\begin{proof}
1094	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
1095	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
1096	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
1097	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
1098	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
1099	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
1100	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
1101	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
1102	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
1103	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
1104	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
1105	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
1106	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
1107	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
1108	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
1109	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
1110	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
1111	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
1112	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
1113	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
1114	  \[
1115	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
1116	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
1117	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
1118	  \]
1119	  where the second equality uses
1120	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
1121	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
1122	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
1123	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
1124	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
1125	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
1126	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
1127	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
1128	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
1129	  \[
1130	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
1131	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
1132	  \]
1133	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
1134	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
1135	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
1136	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
1137	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
1138	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
1139	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
1140	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
1141	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
1142	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
1143	  Therefore
1144	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
1145	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
1146	  taking degree completes the proof.
1147	\end{proof}
1148	\section{Divisor class group}
1149	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
1150	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
1151	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
1152	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
1153	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
1154	out of scope here.
1155	\begin{definition}
1156	\leanok
1157	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
1158	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
1159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
1160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
1161	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
1162	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
1163	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
1164	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
1165	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
1166	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
1167	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
1168	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
1169	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
1170	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
1171	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
1172	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
1173	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
1174	  \[
1175	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
1176	  \]
1177	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
1178	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
1179	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
1180	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
1181	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
1182	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
1183	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
1184	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
1185	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
1186	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
45	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for \(H^i(C, \mathcal F)\) on a proper \(k\)-curve.} Mathlib's \(\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}\) provides \(\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\) for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes \(\mathtt{def\ genus}\), not \(\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}\). Because \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\), \(\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}\), and the structure-sheaf-as-\(k\)-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the \(\mathtt{noncomputable}\) modifier on the \(\mathtt{def}\). The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem \(\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) in \(\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}\) states that the Jacobian of \(C\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\) --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.

982	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
983	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
984	
985	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
986	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
987	point.
988	
989	\begin{theorem}
990	
991	\leanok
992	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
993	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
994	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
995	  
996	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
997	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
998	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
999	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
1000	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
1001	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
1002	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
1003	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
1004	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
1005	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
1006	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1007	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
1008	
1009	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1010	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
1011	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
1012	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
1013	  in \(X\).
1014	\end{theorem}
1015	
1016	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
1017	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
1018	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
1019	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
1020	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
1021	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
1022	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
1023	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
1024	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
1025	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
1026	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
1027	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
1028	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
1029	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
1030	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
1031	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
1032	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
1033	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
1034	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
1035	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
1036	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
1037	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
1038	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
1039	% which is a contradiction."
1040	\begin{proof}
1041	  
1042	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
1043	
1044	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
1045	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
1046	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
1047	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
1048	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
1049	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
1050	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
1051	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
1052	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
1053	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
1054	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
1055	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
1056	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
1057	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
1058	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
1059	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
1060	
1061	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
1062	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
1063	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
1064	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
1065	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
1066	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
1067	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
1068	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
1069	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
1070	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
1071	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
1072	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
1073	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
1074	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
1075	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
1076	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
1077	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
1078	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
1079	
1080	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
1081	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
1082	  agree on all of \(X\).
1083	\end{proof}
1084	
1085	\begin{remark}
1086	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
1087	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
1088	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
1089	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
1090	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
1091	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
1092	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
1093	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
1094	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
1095	
1096	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
1097	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
1098	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
1099	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
1100	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
1101	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
1102	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
1103	\end{remark}
1104	
1105	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
1106	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
1107	
1108	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
1109	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
1110	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
1111	this forces the locus to be empty.
1112	
1113	\begin{lemma}
1114	
1115	\leanok
1116	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
1117	  variety]
1118	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
1119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
1120	  
1121	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
1122	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
1123	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
1124	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
1125	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
1126	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
1127	  % divisors).}"
1128	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
1129	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
1130	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
1131	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
1132	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
1133	  union of prime divisors).
1134	
1135	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
1136	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
1137	\end{lemma}
1138	
1139	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
1140	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
1141	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
1142	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
1143	% map represented by
1144	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
1145	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
1146	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
1147	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
1148	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
1149	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
1150	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
1151	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
1152	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
1153	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
1154	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
1155	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
1156	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
1157	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
1158	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
1159	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
1160	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
1161	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
1162	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
1163	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
1164	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
1165	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
1166	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
1167	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
1168	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
1169	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
1170	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
1171	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
1172	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
1173	\begin{proof}
1174	  
1175	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
1176	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
1177	
1178	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
1179	  \[
1180	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
1181	    \qquad
1182	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
1183	  \]
1184	  via the composition
1185	  \[
1186	    U \times U
1187	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
1188	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
1189	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
1190	  \]
1191	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
1192	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
1193	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
1194	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
1195	
1196	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
1197	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
1198	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
1199	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
1200	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
1201	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
1202	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
1203	  identity
1204	  \[
1205	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
1206	    \qquad u \in W',
1207	  \]
1208	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
1209	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
1210	
1211	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
1212	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
1213	  \(K(X \times X)\):
1214	  \[
1215	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
1216	  \]
1217	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
1218	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
1219	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
1220	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
1221	
1222	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
1223	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
1224	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
1225	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
1226	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
1227	  \[
1228	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
1229	  \]
1230	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
1231	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
1232	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
1233	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
1234	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
1235	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
1236	  \[
1237	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
1238	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
1239	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1240	  \]
1241	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1242	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1243	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1244	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1245	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1246	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1247	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1248	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1249	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1250	
1251	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1252	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
1253	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
1254	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1255	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
1256	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1257	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1258	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1259	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1260	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1261	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1262	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1263	\end{proof}
1264	
1265	\begin{remark}
1266	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1267	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1268	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1269	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1270	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1271	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1272	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1273	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1274	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1275	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1276	\end{remark}

1278	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1279	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1280	
1281	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1282	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1283	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1284	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1285	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1286	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1287	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1288	
1289	\begin{theorem}
1290	
1291	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
1292	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1293

1323	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
1324	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
1325	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
1326	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
1327	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
1328	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
1329	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
1330	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
1331	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
1332	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
1333	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
1334	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
1335	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
1336	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
1337	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
1338	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
1339	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
1340	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
1341	  \begin{enumerate}
1342	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
1343	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
1344	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
1345	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
1346	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
1347	      satisfying
1348	      \[
1349	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
1350	      \]
1351	  \end{enumerate}
1352	
1353	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1354	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
1355	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
1356	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
1357	\end{theorem}
1358	
1359	\begin{proof}
1360	  
1361	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
1362	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
1363	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
1364	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
1365	  \[
1366	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
1367	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1368	  \]
1369	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
1370	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
1371	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
1372	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
1373	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
1374	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
1375	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
1376	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
1377	
1378	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
1379	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
1380	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
1381	\end{proof}
1382	
1383	\begin{remark}
1384	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
1385	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
1386	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
1387	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
1388	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
1389	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1390	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
1391	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
1392	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
1393	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
1394	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
1395	\end{remark}
1396	
1397	\begin{lemma}
1398	
1399	\leanok
1400	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
1401	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
1402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
1403	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
1404	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
1405	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
1406	  following are equivalent:
1407	  \begin{enumerate}
1408	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
1409	      \(W\)).
1410	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
1411	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
1412	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
1413	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
1414	  \end{enumerate}
1415	\end{lemma}
1416	
1417	\begin{proof}
1418	
1419	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
1420	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
1421	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
1422	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
1423	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
1424	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
1425	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
1426	  the unfolded definition.
1427	\end{proof}
1428	
1429	\begin{remark}
1430	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
1431	  \textbf{Relationship to \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
1432	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
1433	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
1434	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
1435	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
1436	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
1437	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
1438	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
1439	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
1440	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
1441	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
1442	  history.
1443	\end{remark}

1557	\section{Out of scope}
1558	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1559	
1560	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1561	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1562	scope:
1563	\begin{itemize}
1564	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1565	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1566	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1567	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1568	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1569	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1570	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1571	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1572	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1573	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1574	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1575	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1576	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1577	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1578	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1579	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1580	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1581	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1582	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1583	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1584	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1585	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1586	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1587	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1588	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1589	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1590	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1591	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1592	    introduced.
1593	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1594	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1595	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1596	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1597	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1598	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1599	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
1600	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1601	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1602	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1603	\end{itemize}

4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).

37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)

49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).

52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation

69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures

76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\

85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

322	  \label{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one}

332	  equivalence chain \texttt{HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv}
333	  \(\to\) \texttt{constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}
334	  \(\to\) \texttt{SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top}, followed by

339	\begin{lemma}
340	
341	\leanok
342	[H\(^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes]
343	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}

362	
363	\begin{lemma}\leanok
364	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
365	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
367	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
368	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
369	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
370	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
371	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
372	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
373	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
374	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
375	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
376	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
377	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
378	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
379	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
380	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
381	  \[
382	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
383	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
384	  \]
385	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
386	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
387	  \[
388	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
389	  \]
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
394	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
395	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
396	  \leanok
397	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
398	  (REF),
399	  \[
400	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
401	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
402	  \]
403	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements
404	  (\cref{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one} and
405	  \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}), attacked separately
406	  by the prover.
407	
408	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
409	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
410	  \[
411	    H^0(C, k(P))
412	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
413	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
414	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
415	      k(P)
416	    \bigr)
417	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
418	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
419	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
420	    (k(P))(C),
421	  \]
422	  realised by the project lemmas
423	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
424	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
425	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
426	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
427	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
428	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
429	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
430	  goal to
431	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
432	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
433	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
434	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
435	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
436	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
437	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
438	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
439	
440	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
441	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
442	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
443	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
444	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
445	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
446	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
447	
448	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
449	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
450	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
451	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
452	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
453	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
454	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
455	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
456	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
457	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
458	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
459	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
460	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
461	  % gap to clear.
462	
463	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
464	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
465	\end{proof}
