467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
634	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
635	  specific additivity statement
636	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}
640	
641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
709	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
726	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\section{Out of scope}
732	
733	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
734	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
735	
736	\begin{itemize}
737	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
738	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
739	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
740	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
741	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
742	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
743	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
744	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
745	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
746	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
747	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
748	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
749	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
750	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
751	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
752	    of REF the dependency on
753	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
754	    exact sequence of the closed point.
755	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
756	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
757	    premise by REF; its closure is
758	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
759	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
760	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
761	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
762	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
763	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
764	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
765	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
766	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
767	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
768	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
769	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
770	    principal-divisor identity REF from
771	    \texttt{RR.1}.
772	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
773	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
774	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
775	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
776	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
777	    curve specialisation.
778	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	% --- iter-187 plan-phase additions: pins for the iter-187 refactor's
102	% Step 3 + Step 4 sub-helpers (carrierPresheaf functor + sheaf property)
103	% which are the direct-construction substrate the definition below
104	% consumes (via the iter-186 plan-phase `analogies/ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`

110	\begin{definition}
111	\leanok
112	[Presheaf carrier for the line bundle of a closed point]
113	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf}
115	  For each open \(U \subseteq C\), the \(\bar k\)-submodule
116	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \subset K(C)\) of section candidates
117	  satisfying the order conditions ``\(\mathrm{ord}_Q f \ge 0\) for all
118	  prime divisors \(Q \in U\) other than \(P\), and additionally
119	  \(\mathrm{ord}_P f \ge -1\) when \(P \in U\)'' assembles into a
120	  \(\bar k\)-linear functor
121	  \[
122	    \texttt{carrierPresheaf}_P : (\Opens\, |C|)^{\op}
123	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
124	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
125	  \]
126	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
127	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from
128	  the antitone monotonicity \(U \mapsto \mathrm{carrierSet}_P(U)\) (witness
129	  \(\texttt{carrierSet\_mono}\) at L181-192 of the Lean file) by
130	  \(\texttt{ext}\,\langle x, \_\rangle\); \texttt{rfl}.
131	\end{definition}

133	\begin{lemma}
134	\leanok
135	[Sheaf property of the carrier presheaf]
136	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
138	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf,
139	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}

141	  carrier-refinement of \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf})
142	  is a sheaf in the appropriate Grothendieck topology on \(C\). Concretely,
143	  the locality + gluing conditions for \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued sections
144	  reduce (via \(\texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_forget}\)) to the sheaf
145	  condition on the underlying Set-valued functor: each
146	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U)\) is a subset of the constant
147	  \(K(C)\)-sheaf restricted to \(U\), and the sheaf property in the
148	  constant-sheaf base case is direct. The project's

154	\begin{definition}
155	
156	\leanok
157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
173	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
174	  \]
175	  forces \(F(\bot) = \bot\) (intersection annihilates) while preserving
176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).
177	  The case-based restriction map (zero target on \(\bot\); inclusion
178	  otherwise) gives a well-formed sheaf-condition substrate consumed by
179	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}. Downstream
180	  consumers \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, \texttt{toFunctionField},
181	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_*} are updated to supply the
182	  \texttt{trivAtBot} witness at \(\op\,\top\) via the \texttt{htop\_ne\_bot}
183	  helper.

186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}

275

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  
393	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
394	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
395	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
396	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
397	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
398	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
399	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
400	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
401	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
402	  and only if:
403	
404	  \begin{itemize}
405	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
406	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
407	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
408	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
409	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
410	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
411	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
412	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
413	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
414	  \end{itemize}
415	  In divisorial language (REF,
416	  REF), the two conditions combine to
417	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
418	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
419	  zero global section.
420	
421	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
422	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
423	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
424	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
425	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
426	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
427	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
428	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
429	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
430	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
431	\end{proof}

433	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
440	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
442	  
443	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
444	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
445	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
446	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
447	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
448	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
450	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
451	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
452	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
453	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
454	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
455	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
456	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
457	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
458	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
459	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
460	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
461	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
462	  \[
463	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
464	  \]
465	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  
470	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
471	  exact sequence
472	  \[
473	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
474	  \]
475	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
476	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
477	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
478	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
479	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
480	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
481	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
482	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
483	  \[
484	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
485	  \]
486	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
487	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
488	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
489	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
490	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
491	  \begin{multline*}
492	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
493	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
494	  \end{multline*}
495	  Two ingredients close the proof:
496	
497	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
498	  hypothesis: by REF,
499	  \[
500	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
501	  \]
502	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
503	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
504	
505	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
506	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
507	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
508	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
509	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
510	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
511	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
512	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
513	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
514	
515	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
516	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
517	  \[
518	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
519	  \]
520	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
523	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
524	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
525	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
526	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
527	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
528	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
529	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
530	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
531	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
532	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
533	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
534	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
535	  complex degenerates).
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
539	
540	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
541	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
542	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
543	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
544	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
545	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
546	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
547	% reading the body should consult those.
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	
551	\leanok
552	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
553	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
554	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
555	  
556	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
557	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
558	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
559	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
560	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
561	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
562	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
563	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
564	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
565	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
566	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
567	  % is the Euler characteristic
568	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
569	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
570	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
571	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
572	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
573	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
574	  \[
575	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
576	  \]
577	\end{theorem}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  
581	  
582	  
583	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
584	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
585	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
586	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
587	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
588	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
589	  \[
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
591	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
592	  \]
593	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
594	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
595	  \[
596	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
597	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
598	    \;=\; 2.
599	  \]
600	  By
601	  REF, the second
602	  term vanishes:
603	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
604	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
605	
606	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
607	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
608	  REF computes
609	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
610	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
611	  \[
612	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
613	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
614	  \]
615	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
616	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
617	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
618	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
619	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
620	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
621	  segment yields the short exact sequence
622	  \[
623	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
624	  \]
625	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
626	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
627	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
628	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses

664	\begin{lemma}

692	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}

700	      the project's $\bar k$-linear isomorphism

703	        \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;

706	      bridge is the project-substrate

714	      this step is injective on hom-sets.

716	      Mathlib's constant-sheaf-adjunction
717	      \(\texttt{constantSheaf} \dashv \Gamma\) supplies the
718	      \(\bar k\)-linear isomorphism
719	      $\Hom_{\Psh}(\mathbf 1_{\mathrm{psh}}, \mathcal O_C(P).\val)
720	       \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
721	       \Hom_{\ModuleCat\,\bar k}(\bar k, \mathcal O_C(P).\val(\top))$.
722	    \item Evaluation at the generator \(1\in\bar k\): the canonical
723	      \(\bar k\)-linear isomorphism
724	      $\Hom_{\ModuleCat\,\bar k}(\bar k, M)
725	       \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
726	       M, \qquad \varphi \mapsto \varphi(1),$

870	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
871	  This is the classical Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement
872	  ``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete geometrically
873	  irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically closed field
874	  \(\bar k\)'', combined with the
875	  ``no-zeros-no-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' content of
876	  Stacks~\texttt{02P0}, in two ingredients:
877	
878	  \emph{Step (i) — algebraic Hartogs.} On the normal Noetherian scheme
879	  \(C\), a rational function regular at every codimension-\(1\) point
880	  extends uniquely to a global section of the structure sheaf
881	  (Stacks tag~\texttt{0BCK}: $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) =
882	  \bigcap_{Q\in C^{(1)}} \mathcal O_{C, Q}$). On the smooth curve
883	  \(C\) every prime divisor \(Q\) is a codimension-\(1\) point and
884	  \(\ord_Q\) is the standard discrete valuation on
885	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\); the hypothesis \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every
886	  \(Q\) places \(f\) in the intersection
887	  \(\bigcap_Q \mathcal O_{C, Q} = \Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\).
888	
889	  \emph{Step (ii) — global sections are constants.} On a proper
890	  geometrically irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically
891	  closed field \(\bar k\), the global-sections ring of the structure
892	  sheaf coincides with the base field,
893	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4; or
894	  cohomologically: \(\Gamma\) is finite-dimensional over \(\bar k\)
895	  by Hartshorne~III.5.2, hence integral over \(\bar k\), hence
896	  algebraic, hence \(\bar k\) by algebraic closure). Combining
897	  step~(i) with step~(ii) places \(f\) in the image of the constant
898	  inclusion \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\), as required.
899	
900	  \emph{Mathlib status.} At snapshot \texttt{b80f227} both ingredients
901	  are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps: the Lean substrate helper carries
902	  a single typed sorry pending upstream development; the named-helper
903	  extraction makes the upstream dependency explicit for future
904	  iterations.
905	\end{proof}

907	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
908	
909	\begin{corollary}
910	
911	
912	\leanok
913	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
914	single simple pole]
915	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
916	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
917	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
918	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
919	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
920	  
921	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
922	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
923	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
924	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
925	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
926	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
927	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
928	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
929	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
930	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
931	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
932	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
933	  \begin{itemize}
934	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
935	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
936	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
937	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
938	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
939	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
940	  \end{itemize}
941	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
942	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
943	\end{corollary}
944	
945	\begin{proof}
946	  
947	  
948	  
949	  By
950	  REF,
951	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
952	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
953	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
954	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
955	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
956	  identification of
957	  REF), is a one-dimensional
958	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
959	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
960	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
961	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
962	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
963	
964	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
965	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
966	  REF the section
967	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
968	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
969	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
970	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
971	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
972	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
973	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
974	  of the statement are now immediate.
975	
976	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
977	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
978	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
979	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
980	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
981	
982	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
983	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
984	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
985	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
986	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
987	  REF's output. The
988	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
989	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
990	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
991	\end{proof}

1046	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters or queued for

1048	
1049	\begin{itemize}
1050	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
1051	    associated to an arbitrary Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
1052	    \mathrm{Div}(C)$, and the linear-equivalence isomorphism
1053	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), are
1054	    \emph{not} performed in this chapter. The chapter constructs only
1055	    the specialisation \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) a prime divisor,
1056	    which is the only case consumed by the genus-\(0\) classification
1057	    chain. The general \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is queued for the
1058	    post-RR-bridge follow-up (where it pairs with the general-\(g\)
1059	    Riemann--Roch formula and the canonical-divisor identification).
1060	  \item The general-\(g\) statement
1061	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = \deg([P]) + 1 -
1062	    g(C) + \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, while a direct
1063	    consequence of
1064	    REF of \texttt{RR.2},
1065	    is not separately formalised here; the chapter restricts to the
1066	    genus-\(0\) specialisation \(g = 0\) on which
1067	    REF is
1068	    available.
1069	  \item \textbf{Serre duality} $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong
1070	    H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$ and the associated
1071	    canonical divisor \(K_C = c_1(\omega_C)\) are \emph{not} used at any
1072	    step of this chapter and are not deliverables; the chapter routes
1073	    cohomological vanishing through the long exact sequence at
1074	    \(D = 0\) (REF)
1075	    instead. Serre duality is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
1076	  \item The morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) produced by applying
1077	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} to the basis \((1, f)\) of
1078	    REF,
1079	    and its identification as an isomorphism, are the content of
1080	    \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
1081	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The present
1082	    chapter supplies the basis \((1, f)\) and stops.
1083	  \item The general-\(g\) vanishing
1084	    \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) for \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\) is
1085	    \emph{not} part of this chapter and is not required for the
1086	    genus-\(0\) critical path. It is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement
1087	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
1088	  \item The interpretation of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as the space
1089	    of sections of a complete linear system \(|[P]|\) in
1090	    projective-space form (Hartshorne~II.7.7) is gestured at by
1091	    REF but the
1092	    projective-space structure on the underlying set is not separately
1093	    formalised; the chapter consumes only the \(\bar k\)-vector space
1094	    structure on \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
1095	\end{itemize}
