219	  % documented fallback if Route A blocks on a Mathlib gap.
220	\end{proof}
221	
222	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The companion Lean substrate helper
223	% `Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` (the
224	% Hartshorne~II.1~Exercise~1.16(b) consequence packaged here as the
225	% surjectivity hypothesis) was structurally decomposed iter-194 via a
226	% `forget₂` bridge to `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`: 3 axiom-clean substrate
227	% helpers landed (`sheafCompose_additive`,
228	% `sheafCompose_preservesZero`, `Scheme.IsFlasque.toAddCommGrpCat`);
229	% `Mono SAb.f` + `Epi SAb.g` are now proven axiom-clean inline.  Only
230	% the typeclass `(sheafCompose (forget₂ ModuleCat AddCommGrpCat))%
231	% .PreservesHomology` remains as the focused residual gap; iter-195
232	% closure target.
233	\begin{lemma}
234	
235	\leanok
236	[Cokernel of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence is flasque]
237	  \label{lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque}
239	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
240	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
241	  % Hartshorne~II.1~Exercise~1.16(c) / III.2.5 chain (iter-193 Lane H
242	  % prover-side construction). The classical reference is Hartshorne
243	  % II.1, Exercise~1.16(c); see the verbatim quote in the proof of
244	  % \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} below.
245	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate (Hartshorne~II.1,
246	  Exercise~1.16(c) packaged with the sections-surjectivity input from
247	  II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) as a hypothesis).}
248	  Let $X$ be a topological space and let
249	  \[
250	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
251	  \]
252	  be a short exact sequence in
253	  $\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
254	  (\Module \bar k)$. Suppose:
255	  \begin{enumerate}
256	    \item $\mathcal F_2$ is flasque (in the sense of
257	      \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}); and
258	    \item the section-level map
259	      $\mathcal F_2(U) \to \mathcal F_3(U)$ is surjective for every open
260	      $U \subseteq X$ (the Hartshorne~II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) input,
261	      packaged as a hypothesis rather than invoked internally — this
262	      keeps the lemma's axiom-set clean and lets the consumer site
263	      supply the surjectivity from its own substrate).
264	  \end{enumerate}
265	  Then the cokernel sheaf $\mathcal F_3$ is flasque.
266	
267	  Concretely, given $V \le U$ in $\Opens X$ and a section
268	  $t \in \mathcal F_3(V)$: lift $t$ to $\tilde t \in \mathcal F_2(V)$
269	  via hypothesis~(2) at $V$, extend $\tilde t$ to
270	  $\tilde T \in \mathcal F_2(U)$ via flasqueness of $\mathcal F_2$, and
271	  push $\tilde T$ through the map $\mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3$ at
272	  $U$. Naturality of the morphism on opens identifies the restriction
273	  of the resulting section in $\mathcal F_3(U)$ with the original $t$.
274	\end{lemma}

276	\begin{lemma}
277	
278	\leanok
279	[$\Ext^{n+1}$ vanishes from $\Ext^n$ vanishing on the rightmost term, with injective middle term]
280	  \label{lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero}
282	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
283	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
284	  % Hartshorne~III.2.5 induction (iter-193 Lane H prover-side
285	  % construction). The underlying mathematical statement is the
286	  % classical degree-shifting argument that, for an injective middle
287	  % term in a short exact sequence, the connecting morphism in the
288	  % long exact $\Ext$ sequence is surjective above the injective
289	  % dimension. No external source is cited verbatim.
290	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate.}
291	  Let $\mathcal C$ be an abelian category equipped with a
292	  \texttt{HasExt} instance, let $X$ be an object of $\mathcal C$, and
293	  let
294	  \[
295	    0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0
296	  \]
297	  be a short exact sequence in $\mathcal C$ whose middle term $S_2$ is
298	  injective. Let $n_0 \ge 1$ be a natural number and assume that
299	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ (i.e.\ every element of $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3)$
300	  vanishes). Then $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1) = 0$.
301	
302	  The proof is the classical degree-shift. Since $S_2$ is injective and
303	  $n_0 + 1 \ge 2$, the injective-dimension subsingleton instance forces
304	  $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_2)$ to be subsingleton; combined with exactness
305	  of the covariant $\Ext$ long exact sequence at $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X,
306	  S_1)$, every $x_1 \in \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$ is in the image of the
307	  connecting morphism $\delta \colon \Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
308	  \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$. The hypothesis
309	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ then gives $x_1 = 0$. The lemma is consumed
310	  in the $i \ge 2$ inductive case of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}.
311	\end{lemma}

313	\begin{lemma}\leanok
314	[Injective objects of $\mathrm{Sheaf}(X, \Module \bar k)$ are flasque]
315	  \label{lem:isFlasque_injective}
316	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque}
317	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
318	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Lemma~2.4 (read from
319	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf — the project's local
320	  % .md transcript does not extend to this passage, so no verbatim quote
321	  % is pasted in this block per the iter-197 directive; the citation
322	  % pointer is recorded for downstream retrieval).
323	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Lemma~2.4.}
324	
325	  Let \(I\) be an injective object of
326	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
327	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then \(I\) is flasque in the sense of
328	  \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}: for every inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) of
329	  opens of \(X\), the restriction map
330	  \(I(U) \to I(V)\) is surjective on underlying \(\bar k\)-modules.
331	
332	  The classical argument (Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4) uses the
333	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\) functor: for an open inclusion
334	  \(j_V \colon V \hookrightarrow X\), one has the short exact sequence
335	  \(0 \to j_{V!}\mathcal O_V \to j_{U!}\mathcal O_U \to
336	  j_{U!}\mathcal O_U / j_{V!}\mathcal O_V \to 0\), and injectivity of
337	  \(I\) makes the induced
338	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(j_{U!}\mathcal O_U, I) \to
339	    \mathrm{Hom}(j_{V!}\mathcal O_V, I)\) surjective. Translating
340	  through the \(j_{(-)!} \dashv j_{(-)}^*\) adjunction (or the
341	  equivalent presheaf form for the constant ring sheaf \(\bar k\))
342	  identifies the two Hom-sets with \(I(U)\) and \(I(V)\) respectively,
343	  delivering the surjective restriction map.

345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_pushforward}

368	\begin{theorem}
369	
370	\leanok
371	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
372	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
374	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_injective,
375	        lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact,
376	        lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
377	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
378	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
379	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
380	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
381	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
382	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
383	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
384	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
385	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
386	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
387	  \[
388	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
389	  \]
390	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
391	\end{theorem}
392	
393	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
394	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
395	% be the quotient:
396	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
397	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
398	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
399	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
400	% Ex. 1.16b)
401	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
402	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
403	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
404	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
405	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
406	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
407	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
408	% \(i\) we get the result."
409	\begin{proof}
410	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
411	  \leanok
412	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
413	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
414	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
415	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
416	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
417	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
418	  short exact sequence
419	  \[
420	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
421	  \]
422	  in the sheaf category.
423	
424	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
425	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
426	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
427	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
428	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
429	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
430	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
431	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
432	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
433	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
434	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
435	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
436	
437	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
438	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
439	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
440	  exact sequence on global sections
441	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
442	  \[
443	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
444	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
445	  \]
446	
447	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
448	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
449	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
450	  positive degree:
451	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
452	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
453	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
454	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
455	  \[
456	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
457	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
458	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
459	  \]
460	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
461	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
462	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
463	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
464	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
465	
466	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
467	  exact sequence yields
468	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
469	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
470	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
471	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
472	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
473	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
474	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
475	
476	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
477	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
478	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
479	  % already provided by chapter
480	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
481	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
482	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
483	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
484	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
485	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
486	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
487	  % LOC apiece.
488	\end{proof}

490	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
491	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
492	
493	\begin{lemma}
494	
495	\leanok
496	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
497	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
498	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
499	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
500	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
501	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
502	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
503	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
504	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
505	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
506	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
507	  % X\) is the inclusion."
508	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
509	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
510	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
511	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
512	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
513	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is isomorphic to the
514	  pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
515	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
516	
517	  % NOTE (iter-197 lean-vs-blueprint-checker M-2): The Lean signature
518	  % `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` (file
519	  % `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean`, L818) is
520	  % materially weaker than the mathematical statement displayed above:
521	  % the Lean conclusion is
522	  %     `Nonempty (skyscraperSheaf P A ≅
523	  %        (pushforward _ _).obj ((constantSheaf _ _).obj A))`,
524	  % i.e.\ the existence of an isomorphism rather than a concrete one.
525	  % The `Nonempty` wrapper is forced by the use of `Classical.choice`
526	  % to unpack the inner iso of
527	  % \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}, which itself
528	  % carries a typed sorry pending the Mathlib gap discussed there.
529	  % When that inner iso is constructed explicitly, the `Nonempty`
530	  % wrapper can be removed and the present declaration upgraded to
531	  % a direct concrete-iso statement (no consumer change required at
532	  % the headline `H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` because it
533	  % does not extract iso morphisms from this lemma).
534	\end{lemma}
535	
536	\begin{proof}
537	  \uses{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
538	  \leanok
539	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
540	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
541	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
542	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
543	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
544	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
545	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
546	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
547	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
548	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
549	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
550	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
551	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
552	
553	  In the Lean formalisation this argument is split into two pieces.
554	  The \emph{outer} step is the presheaf-level equality
555	  \[
556	    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A \;=\;
557	      \bigl(i_*\;\bigr)
558	      \bigl(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A\bigr)
559	  \]
560	  obtained from Mathlib's \texttt{skyscraperPresheaf\_eq\_pushforward}
561	  via \texttt{ObjectProperty.FullSubcategory.ext} (axiom-clean, no
562	  Mathlib gap). The \emph{inner} step is the iso
563	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A \cong
564	    (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit})\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A\)
565	  on the one-point space \(\PUnit\); this is the actual blocking
566	  technical step and is isolated as
567	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit} below. Composing
568	  the two pieces (with the inner iso unpacked via
569	  \texttt{Classical.choice} from its current \(\mathtt{Nonempty}\)
570	  wrapper) gives the lemma.
571	\end{proof}
572	
573	\begin{lemma}\leanok
574	[Inner iso: skyscraperSheaf vs.\ constantSheaf on $\PUnit$]
575	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
577	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
578	  \textit{Project-bespoke sub-lemma} (Lean-substrate technical step
579	  for \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}; no separate external
580	  source — the underlying mathematical content is the one-point case
581	  of Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.17 quoted in
582	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}).
583	
584	  Let \(\PUnit\) denote the one-point topological space, let
585	  \(J_{\PUnit}\) denote the Grothendieck topology of opens on
586	  \(\PUnit\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module. Then there is an
587	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(\PUnit\)
588	  \[
589	    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A \;\cong\;
590	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k)\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A.
591	  \]
592	\end{lemma}
593	
594	\begin{proof}
595	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
596	  \leanok
597	  The space \(\PUnit\) has exactly two opens, \(\bot\) (empty) and
598	  \(\top\) (the whole space, which contains \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\)).
599	  We build the iso pointwise on these two opens and check naturality
600	  on the unique non-identity inclusion \(\bot \le \top\).
601	
602	  \emph{Forward map.} Apply Mathlib's
603	  \(\mathtt{constantSheafAdj}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k\;
604	   h_{T\top}\,.\,\mathtt{homEquiv}\,.\,\mathtt{symm}\) to the natural
605	  identification
606	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A\,.\,\mathrm{val}\,.
607	    \,\mathrm{obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;\top) = A\),
608	  which holds by \(\mathtt{simp}\;[\mathtt{skyscraperPresheaf}]\) since
609	  \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit} \in \top\).
610	
611	  \emph{Inverse map.} Build pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\):
612	  \begin{itemize}
613	    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\bot\): both sheaves give the terminal
614	      object in \(\Module \bar k\) (the constant sheaf is the
615	      sheafification of the constant presheaf, whose value at the
616	      empty open is the zero module after sheafification; the
617	      skyscraper sheaf at the empty open is by construction
618	      \(0\)). Use \(\mathtt{IsTerminal.uniqueUpToIso}\) to identify
619	      them;
620	    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\): use the constantSheaf-unit-iso on
621	      \(\PUnit\), which is true since the \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\)
622	      functor is fully faithful on \(\PUnit\) (this fully-faithful
623	      instance is the Mathlib gap described below in the route
624	      choice).
625	  \end{itemize}
626	
627	  \emph{Naturality} on the unique non-identity inclusion
628	  \(\bot \le \top\): both sides land in the terminal object at
629	  \(\bot\), so commutativity is automatic by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton}\)
630	  of the terminal object.
631	
632	  \paragraph{Mathlib gap (route choice).} The inverse map's
633	  \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\) component needs that the
634	  \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\) functor on \(\PUnit\) is fully faithful.
635	  Two routes close the gap; both are tracked for the iter-197+
636	  prover:
637	  \begin{itemize}
638	    \item \emph{Route A} (preferred — Mathlib upstreaming
639	      candidate): provide instances
640	      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
641	      \(.\) \texttt{Full} and
642	      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
643	      \(.\) \texttt{Faithful}. Likely the smallest project-side
644	      change once a Mathlib PR for the analogous fact for the
645	      one-point space is accepted; the inverse map then follows by
646	      \texttt{Functor.preimage} of the forward map.
647	    \item \emph{Route B} (fallback — no Mathlib upstream): directly
648	      construct the iso pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\) as
649	      above, using \(\mathtt{Sheaf.IsTerminal}\) at \(\bot\) and an
650	      explicit value extraction at \(\top\) (the constant sheaf at
651	      the unique non-empty open of an irreducible space is the
652	      identity on \(A\) after sheafification — same fact as
653	      \cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible} for the non-empty
654	      branch, used here only at the one-point space). Estimated

656	  \end{itemize}

659	\begin{lemma}
660	
661	\leanok
662	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
663	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
665	
666	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
667	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
668	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
669	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
670	
671	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
672	  no external source pinned).
673	\end{lemma}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	
677	
678	\leanok
679	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
680	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
681	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
682	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
683	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
684	\end{proof}

741	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
742	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
743	
744	\begin{theorem}
745	
746	\leanok
747	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
748	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
750	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
751	
752	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
753	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
754	  \[
755	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
756	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
757	  \]
758	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
759	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
760	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
761	
762	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
763	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
764	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
765	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
766	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
767	  above.
768	\end{theorem}
769	
770	\begin{proof}
771	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
772	  \leanok
773	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
774	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
775	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
776	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
777	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
778	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
779	  \[
780	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
781	  \]
782	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
783	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
784	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The relative cotangent presheaf}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the relative cotangent \emph{presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of a morphism of schemes \(f \colon X \to S\), starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) already available in Mathlib. The output is a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules whose sections on each open \(V \subseteq X\) are the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\), where \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\) denotes the \emph{presheaf-level} inverse-image functor (left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\)).
5	
6	The downstream role of this chapter is to provide the \emph{forward direction} of the standard smoothness criterion: smoothness of \(f\) implies that on every affine chart \(V \to U\) (with \(V \subseteq X\), \(U \subseteq S\) affine and \(f V \subseteq U\)) the appLE-algebra K\"ahler differential \(\Omega_{\Gamma(X, V)\,/\,\Gamma(S, U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\). This is the Jacobian-criterion direction and is what connects to the dimension hypothesis on the Jacobian: the smoothness of the curve \(C \to \Spec k\) in the Jacobian definition is the special case \(n = 1\), and the relative dimension of \(\Jac(C)\) is read off from the rank via the same forward implication. The bridge between this affine-chart-wise algebra-K\"ahler form and the section module of the relative cotangent presheaf \(\Omega_{X/S}\) was the former content of milestone~M1; the bridge declaration was excised iter-126 (zero in-tree consumers) and replaced in this chapter by the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities of Section~REF, the off-loop Mathlib-PR work of which proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}. The converse implication of the forward smoothness criterion is \emph{mathematically false} as stated and is documented separately in Section~REF.
7	
8	\section{The relative cotangent presheaf}
9	
10	\begin{definition}
11	
12	\leanok
13	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
14	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
15	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
16	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes. The presheaf-level inverse-image presheaf of rings \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S}\) on \(X\) (the left Kan extension of \(\struct{S}\) along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\), given on each open \(V \subseteq X\) by the colimit \(\mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\)) is paired with \(\struct{X}\) by the canonical adjunction-transpose \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S} \to \struct{X}\) of the comorphism \(f^{\sharp}\colon\struct{S}\to f_{*}\struct{X}\). Applying the presheaf-of-modules K\"ahler differential construction \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\) to this map of presheaves of commutative rings produces a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules on \(X\), the \emph{relative cotangent presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\).
17	\end{definition}
18	
19	\begin{lemma}
20	
21	\leanok
22	[Sections of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on an open]
23	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
24	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler}
25	  
26	  For every open \(V \subseteq X\), the sections of the relative cotangent presheaf are, by construction, the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\):
27	  \[
28	    \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{\struct{X}(V) \,/\, (f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)}.
29	  \]
30	  Here \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) = \mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\) is the presheaf-level inverse-image section ring, \emph{not} \(\struct{S}(U)\) for an arbitrary affine open \(U \supseteq f V\). The relationship between this colimit-source K\"ahler module and the classical ring-theoretic \(\Omega_{B/A}\) for \(A = \struct{S}(U)\), \(B = \struct{X}(V)\) on an affine chart \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}U\) with \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) affine is the content of the bridge documented in Section~REF.
31	\end{lemma}
32	
33	\begin{proof}
34	  
35	  \leanok
36	  By definition of \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\): the construction is built object-wise to be the Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module of the ring map at each open. The identification is therefore by \texttt{rfl} after unfolding the definition. The colimit description of \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\) is the definition of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} as the left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\) in Mathlib.
37	\end{proof}
38	
39	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level \(\Omega_{B/A}\)]
40	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
41	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) together with the universal derivation \(\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}\). The construction of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on each open is, by definition, this Mathlib module applied at the ring level of the presheaf, so all ring-level Mathlib lemmas about \(\Omega_{B/A}\) transfer verbatim under Lemma~REF — but for the \emph{colimit-source} ring \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\), not for \(\struct{S}(U)\). Transferring to the classical \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) source on an affine chart is the bridge of Section~REF.
42	\end{remark}
43	
44	\section{Smoothness criterion for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (forward direction)}
45	
46	The forward direction of the textbook smoothness criterion identifies a necessary condition on the relative K\"ahler differentials: every point of \(X\) admits an affine chart \(V \to U\) over which the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is free of the prescribed rank, with \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) and \(B = \struct{X}(V)\). This is the global version of the algebraic-side fact that an \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~algebra has a free K\"ahler differential module of rank \(n\). We state and prove the theorem directly on the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module, sidestepping the presheaf bridge of Section~REF. The converse implication (a local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) alone implying smoothness) is \emph{false}; see Section~REF.
47	
48	\begin{theorem}\leanok
49	[Smooth implies \(\Omega\) locally free (algebra-K\"ahler form)]
50	  \label{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_locally_free_omega}
52	  
53	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). If \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\), then for every point \(x \in X\) there exist an affine open \(U \subseteq S\), an affine open \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\), such that the K\"ahler differential module
54	  \[
55	    \Omega_{\Gamma(X, V) \,/\, \Gamma(S, U)}
56	  \]
57	  (over the appLE algebra structure \(\Gamma(S, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\) induced by \(f\)) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\).
58	\end{theorem}
59	
60	\begin{proof}
61	  
62	  \leanok
63	  Fix \(x \in X\). We use the abbreviation \(A := \Gamma(S, U)\) and \(B := \Gamma(X, V)\) for the section rings on the affine chart we will produce; the algebra map \(A \to B\) is the appLE map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\) induced by \(f\).
64	
65	  \emph{Step 1: extract a standard-smooth chart at \(x\).} The auto-generated \texttt{mk\_iff} bridge
66	  \[
67	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} \quad [\text{verified}]
68	  \]
69	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth}; produced by the \texttt{@[mk\_iff]} attribute on the class \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}) unfolds the hypothesis \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(f\) into: for every \(x \in X\), there exist affine opens \(U \subseteq S\) and \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \leq f^{-1}U\), such that the induced algebra map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\colon A \to B\) satisfies \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
70	
71	  \emph{Step 2: pass to plain standard-smooth.} The lemma
72	  \[
73	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} \quad [\text{verified}]
74	  \]
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth}) yields \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\) from the relative-dimension-tagged form, unlocking the cotangent-module machinery in the next two steps.
76	
77	  \emph{Step 3: free K\"ahler module on the chart.} The Mathlib instance
78	  \[
79	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
80	  \]
81	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}) synthesises \texttt{Module.Free}~\(B\)~\(\Omega_{B/A}\) from \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\).
82	
83	  \emph{Step 4: pin the rank.} The theorem
84	  \[
85	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
86	  \]
87	  (same file as Step 3) gives \(\rank_B\,\Omega_{B/A} = n\) under the hypotheses \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
88	
89	  \emph{Step 4.5: discharge the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) side condition.} The chart witness \(x \in V\) shows that the open \(V \subseteq X\) is non-empty as a set, so its structure-sheaf section ring \(B = \Gamma(X, V) = \struct{X}(V)\) is non-zero by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (the structure sheaf of a scheme has \texttt{Nontrivial} sections on a non-empty open). This supplies the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) hypothesis required by Step 4.
90	
91	  Combining Steps 1--4.5, the chart \((U, V, e)\) produced by Step 1 satisfies \(x \in V\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,U\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,V\), and under the appLE algebra structure \(A = \Gamma(S, U) \to B = \Gamma(X, V)\), the K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is a free \(B\)-module of rank \(n\). As \(x \in X\) was arbitrary, the statement follows.
92	
93	  This is the forward direction of the Jacobian criterion: Stacks Tag~02G1 (and Hartshorne~II.8, Theorem~II.5.2 in the Noetherian case).
94	
95	  \emph{Mathlib name summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure pieces the proof relies on are
96	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} (Step 1),
97	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} (Step 2),
98	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 3),
99	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 4),
100	  and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (Step 4.5).
101	\end{proof}
102	
103	\begin{remark}[Lean naming]
104	  \label{rem:smooth_class_naming}
105	  The Lean class for ``smooth of relative dimension \(n\)'' is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension}; the spelling \texttt{IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension} that appeared in earlier iterations of this project is a deprecated alias in Mathlib \texttt{b80f227}. The \texttt{\textbackslash lean\{...\}} hint above names the non-deprecated form.
106	\end{remark}

108	\section{Standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities (post-iter-126 M1 excise)}
109	\label{sec:bridge}
110	
111	The presheaf-form \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of Definition~REF and the algebra-K\"ahler form \(\Omega_{B / A}\) on an affine chart of Theorem~REF are connected, on an affine chart, by a canonical \(B\)-linear isomorphism. Earlier iterations of the project (iter-121 through iter-125) hosted this isomorphism in the Lean tree as a \emph{bridge} declaration \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf\_equiv\_kaehler\_appLE} backed by a Mathlib gap-fill sub-lemma \texttt{appLE\_isLocalization} (the load-bearing localization-at-the-image-submonoid claim, packaged as a multi-LOC residual sorry whose closure was concrete but had no in-tree consumer). The forward-direction smoothness criterion (Theorem~REF) closed iter-120 in algebra-K\"ahler form \emph{independently} of the bridge, so the bridge was project-local infrastructure with zero downstream consumer.
112	
113	\textbf{Excise decision (iter-126).} Per the iter-126 strategy-critic CHALLENGE, ratified by the iter-126 user hint endorsing "shortest path qualified by your role is to fill mathlib gaps", the bridge declaration and its load-bearing sub-lemma were excised from the Lean tree iter-126: closing the bridge produced neither a downstream consumer nor a Mathlib PR (the extractable PR candidate is M1.d — Lemma~REF below — and is \emph{independent} of the bridge). The two retained declarations in this section are the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities that survive as upstream-PR-quality material; off-loop PR work proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}.
114	
115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
116	[K\"ahler module of a localization is trivial]
117	  \label{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
119	  Let \(A \to L\) be a localization at a multiplicative subset \(M \subseteq A\). Then \(\Omega_{L / A}\) is subsingleton (the zero \(L\)-module).
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	
124	\leanok
125	  This is a thin re-export of Mathlib's existing infrastructure: \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of\_isLocalization}~\texttt{[verified]} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Unramified.Basic}, line 303) produces the instance \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified}~\(A\)~\(L\) from any \texttt{IsLocalization}~\(M\)~\(L\) predicate; the instance \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.subsingleton\_kaehlerDifferential}~\texttt{[verified]} (same file, lines 57--59) then yields \texttt{Subsingleton}~\((\mathrm{KaehlerDifferential}~A~L)\). Hence the project-side lemma is a two-line re-export (\(\mathtt{haveI} := \mathtt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of\_isLocalization}~M\) \texttt{; infer\_instance}); contra an earlier framing, no Mathlib gap-fill is required for this lemma.
126	\end{proof}
127	
128	\begin{lemma}\leanok
129	[Tower-cancellation equivalence for K\"ahler modules under localization]
130	  \label{lem:kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}
132	  
133	  Let \(A \to L\) be a localization (in the sense of \texttt{IsLocalization}), and let \(L \to B\) be a ring map forming a scalar tower \(A \to L \to B\). Then the canonical \(B\)-linear map \(\Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/L}\) induced by the tower is a \(B\)-linear equivalence.
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  
138	  \leanok
139	  The second fundamental exact sequence of K\"ahler differentials reads
140	  \[
141	    B \otimes_{L} \Omega_{L/A} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{B/A} \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \Omega_{B/L} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
142	  \]
143	  By Lemma~REF, the left term vanishes (since \(\Omega_{L/A}\) is subsingleton and tensoring against it gives the zero module). Therefore the right surjection has zero kernel and is a \(B\)-linear equivalence. The Mathlib closure pieces are \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map}~\texttt{[verified]} (the exact sequence above) and \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map\_surjective}~\texttt{[verified]} (the right-hand surjection), both in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}; the project-side declaration packages these into a \texttt{LinearEquiv} via \texttt{LinearEquiv.ofBijective}. This is the most extractable Mathlib contribution candidate of milestone~M1: a clean generalisation of \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale} to the ``only the lower step is formally unramified'' case, candidate name \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified}.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\begin{remark}[Mathlib contribution candidate (preserved standalone post-excise)]
147	  \label{rem:bridge_mathlib_pr}
148	  Per the analogist's analysis (\texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}, iter-121), the extractable Mathlib contribution candidate is Lemma~REF: a clean generalisation of \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale} to the ``only the lower step is formally unramified'' case (candidate name \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified}, candidate Mathlib home \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}). This lemma survives the iter-126 M1 excise as standalone PR-ready material; the bridge declaration and its scheme-morphism-shaped \texttt{appLE\_isLocalization} sub-lemma were excised iter-126 because they had zero in-tree consumers, but the Mathlib-PR work proceeds off-loop from the analogist's file.
149	\end{remark}
150	
151	\section{Converse direction (milestone M4)}
152	\label{sec:converse-out-of-scope}
153	
154	This direction is recorded as an optional future milestone~M4 (see \texttt{STRATEGY.md}). Closure requires the deformation-theoretic hypothesis \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~\(A\)~\(B\)\texttt{)}, which the project may wire up if a downstream consumer demands the iff form. The remainder of this section documents the obstruction in concrete form: a counterexample showing that without that hypothesis the converse fails, and a comparison with the Mathlib converse lemma at the ring level.
155	
156	The reverse implication
157	\[
158	  \forall x\in X,\ \exists\,U\text{ affine},\ \Omega_{X/S}(U)\text{ free of rank }n
159	  \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f \text{ smooth of relative dimension } n
160	\]
161	is \emph{mathematically false} as stated, even under the auxiliary hypothesis that \(f\) is locally of finite presentation. Local freeness of \(\Omega\) alone does not imply flatness, and flatness (equivalently, formal smoothness via vanishing of the first Andr\'e--Quillen cohomology) is genuine deformation-theoretic content not encoded in the section-module hypothesis. This is why the iff form of the criterion present in earlier iterations was demoted to a forward implication.
162	
163	\begin{remark}[Explicit counterexample]
164	  \label{rem:converse_counterexample}
165	  Let \(k\) be a field and consider the morphism \(f \colon \Spec k \to \Spec k[t]\) induced by the surjection \(k[t] \twoheadrightarrow k\) sending \(t \mapsto 0\). Then \(f\) is locally of finite presentation, its source \(\Spec k\) has a unique affine open (itself), and the K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{k/k[t]} = 0\) is free of rank \(0\) (the trivial \texttt{Module.Basis} indexed by the empty set witnesses this). Yet \(f\) is the closed immersion of a non-open point of \(\Spec k[t]\), hence is not flat and therefore not smooth, so \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(0\)~\(f\) fails. The local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) is therefore strictly weaker than smoothness, and the converse implication of the iff form fails on this very example.
166	\end{remark}
167	
168	\begin{remark}[The Mathlib converse lemma and its hypotheses]
169	  \label{rem:converse_lemma_hypotheses}
170	  Mathlib does provide a converse-direction lemma at the ring level,
171	  \[
172	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.of\_basis\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
173	  \]
174	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothOfFree}). Its three hypotheses make the missing content explicit:
175	  \begin{itemize}
176	    \item \texttt{Algebra.FinitePresentation}~\(A\)~\(B\);
177	    \item \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~\(A\)~\(B\)\texttt{)} --- vanishing of the first Andr\'e--Quillen cohomology, i.e.\ formal smoothness;
178	    \item a basis \(b \colon \texttt{Module.Basis}~I~B~\Omega_{B/A}\) whose range lies in the image of the universal derivation, \(\mathrm{Set.range}\,(\texttt{Algebra.KaehlerDifferential.D}~A~B)\).
179	  \end{itemize}
180	  None of these three is supplied by the section-module hypothesis of the would-be iff. The first-cotangent-vanishing hypothesis in particular is the deformation-theoretic input that distinguishes ``\(\Omega\) locally free'' from ``smooth''; without it, the counterexample of Remark~REF would already break the converse. Establishing \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent}~\(A\)~\(B\)\texttt{)} from project-side hypotheses (e.g.\ via a flatness assumption added to the input) is the work that would close the converse, and is the content of milestone~M4.
181	\end{remark}
182	
183	\begin{remark}[Cross-reference to Stacks 02G1]
184	  \label{rem:stacks_02G1}
185	  The standard textbook biconditional ``\(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d\) iff \(f\) is flat, locally of finite presentation, and \(\Omega_{X/S}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)'' is Stacks Tag~02G1 (and is the Noetherian-case Hartshorne~II.8, Theorem~II.5.2). The flatness conjunct is precisely what is missing from a hypothesis that only asserts local freeness of \(\Omega\); supplying it amounts to the deformation-theoretic content discussed in Remark~REF. The forward implication proved as Theorem~REF above corresponds to the easy direction of the same tag.
186	\end{remark}
187	
188	\section{Content scheduled for later milestones}
189	
190	Each bullet below is a future milestone whose Mathlib infrastructure is not yet available in the pinned snapshot \texttt{b80f227}; inclusion in this list does \emph{not} mean the project has abandoned it. The roadmap progression is: M1 (this iter) \(\to\) M2 / M3 (existence routes) \(\to\) M4 (converse, Section~REF) \(\to\) M5+ (sheafification, cotangent exact sequence, etc., listed here). Each item lists the deferred concept, the missing Mathlib piece, and one cross-reference for forward-looking reinstatement.
191	
192	\begin{itemize}
193	  \item \textbf{Sheaf condition for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (M5).} The relative cotangent presheaf is, on an affine basis, locally isomorphic to Mathlib's quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{\Omega_{B/A}}\) on each affine chart, and the unique-gluing characterisation of the sheaf property holds basis-wise. No off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma packages ``\texttt{Scheme.PresheafOfModules}-sheaf-on-affine-basis \(\Rightarrow\) sheaf on \(X\)'' however; the cofinality descent against \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafOpensLeCover} is the deferred work for this milestone. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.5 (sheaves from affine charts) and Stacks Tag~009H.
194	
195	  \item \textbf{Cotangent exact sequence (M6).} For a composition of morphisms \(X \to Y \to S\), there is an exact sequence of quasi-coherent \(\struct{X}\)-modules
196	  \[
197	    f^{*}\Omega_{Y/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;}\; \Omega_{X/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\beta\;}\;
198	      \Omega_{X/Y} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
199	  \]
200	  The ring-level ingredients (\texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map} and the surjectivity of the right map) are in Mathlib, but the exactness conjunct requires \texttt{SheafOfModules.exact\_iff\_stalkwise} or an equivalent section-wise+sheafification route through homology-preserving instances of the forget functor; both are Mathlib gaps. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.4A and Stacks Tag~01UV.
201	
202	  \item \textbf{Cotangent space at a section (M7).} A corollary of the smoothness criterion above, pulled back along a section \(s \colon S \to X\): if \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\) and \(s\) is a section, then \(s^{*}\Omega_{X/S}\) is a locally free \(\struct{S}\)-module of rank \(n\). The natural Lean framing uses \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback} against the sheaf-bundled \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}\), which was trimmed in iter-117; a presheaf-form analogue against \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullback} would need a separate formulation. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.8, Corollary~8.10.
203	
204	  \item \textbf{Serre-duality genus identity (M8).} On a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C/k\),
205	  \[
206	    \dim_{k} H^{0}(C, \Omega_{C/k}) \;=\; \dim_{k} H^{1}(C, \struct{C}),
207	  \]
208	  both sides being equal to the genus \(g(C)\). Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf, no trace morphism for proper morphisms, and no Zariski coherent cohomology of \(\struct{X}\)-modules, so the equality cannot yet be expressed within the project's Mathlib snapshot. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~III.7 and Stacks Tag~0FVU.
209	\end{itemize}
210

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase). This chapter is a project-bespoke
6	% substrate file landing two reusable lemmas needed to close the Lane B
7	% \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-scaling cocycle (path (III.c) of
8	% chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity) together with two off-target sorries

11	% generic in the geometric situation (the closed-immersion lift lemma is

13	% \texttt{projectiveLineBar} grading) and live in their own file rather
14	% than inline so they can be re-used by the three GmScaling

16	%
17	% The recipe consolidated below was produced by the
18	% \texttt{mathlib-analogist} subagent in iter-189 and is preserved

22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}\leanok
61	[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
62	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
64	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
65	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
66	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
67	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
68	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
69	  \(i\):
70	  \[
71	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
72	    \;\iff\;
73	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
74	  \]
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{proof}
77	
78	
79	\leanok
80	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
81	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
82	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
83	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
84	
85	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
86	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
87	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
88	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
89	
90	  \begin{enumerate}
91	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
92	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
93	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
94	    ideal sheaf, so
95	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
96	    (Mathlib:
97	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
98	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
99	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
100	    The unconditional inclusion
101	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
102	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
103	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
104	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
105	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
106	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
107	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
108	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
109	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
110	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
111	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
112	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
114	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
115	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
116	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
117	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
118	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
119	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
120	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
121	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
122	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
123	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
124	    \[
125	      i.\mathrm{ker}
126	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
128	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
129	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
130	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
131	    \]
132	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
133	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
134	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
135	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
136	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
137	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
138	  \end{enumerate}
139	\end{proof}

141	\section{Tensor reducedness over the algebraic closure}
142	\label{sec:tensor_AwayGm_isDomain}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}\leanok
145	[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)]
146	  \label{thm:gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
148	  Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a
149	  non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the
150	  \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on
151	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\). Then the
152	  tensor product
153	  \[
154	    \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\,\overline{k}\bigr)\,f
155	    \;\otimes_{\overline{k}}\;
156	    \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
157	  \]
158	  is a domain (and hence reduced).
159	\end{theorem}
160	\begin{proof}
161	
162	\leanok
163	  The proof exhibits a canonical isomorphism of \(\overline{k}\)-algebras
164	  identifying the tensor product with the localisation of a
165	  polynomial ring over a domain at a non-zero-divisor; both operations
166	  preserve the domain property.
167	
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	  \item \emph{Domain instance on the homogeneous localisation.}
170	    \(\mathrm{Away}\,f := \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading})\,f\)
171	    embeds via the \texttt{val\_injective} bridge into
172	    \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,f\); since the polynomial ring
173	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\) is a domain
174	    and \(f \ne 0\), the localisation is a domain, and the bridge
175	    transports the property to \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\). (Already used in
176	    \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}; see
177	    \texttt{GmScaling.lean} L734--738 for the precise instance
178	    threading.)
179	  \item \emph{Collapse the tensor with the polynomial factor.}
180	    Recalling
181	    \(\mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
182	       = \mathrm{Localization.Away}(X()\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k})\),
183	    apply \texttt{MvPolynomial.algebraTensorAlgEquiv} to get
184	    \[
185	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k}
186	      \;\cong\;
187	      \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f),
188	    \]
189	    a \(\overline{k}\)-algebra (in fact \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\)-algebra)
190	    isomorphism.
191	  \item \emph{Push the localisation through the tensor.}
192	    Tensoring with the localisation step
193	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k} \to \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}\)
194	    factors through Mathlib's tensor-base-change lemma
195	    \texttt{IsLocalization.Away.tensorRightEquiv}, yielding an
196	    isomorphism
197	    \[
198	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
199	      \;\cong\;
200	      \mathrm{Localization.Away}(\iota(X())\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f))
201	    \]
202	    where \(\iota\) is the canonical algebra map. The threading uses
203	    \texttt{IsScalarTower} compatibility and the
204	    \texttt{Algebra}/\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\) instance on the
205	    tensored polynomial ring.
206	  \item \emph{Right-hand side is a domain.}
207	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f)\) is a
208	    polynomial ring over a domain (Step~1), hence a domain by the
209	    standard instance. The generator \(X()\) is non-zero
210	    (\texttt{MvPolynomial.X\_ne\_zero}), so its powers form a
211	    submonoid of the non-zero-divisors
212	    (\texttt{powers\_le\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_noZeroDivisors}); the
213	    localisation is then a domain by
214	    \texttt{IsLocalization.isDomain\_localization}.
215	  \end{enumerate}
216	
217	  Transferring the domain property along the iso of Step~3 closes the
218	  goal. Reducedness is a direct corollary of being a domain.
219	\end{proof}

17	This chapter introduces the project's named declaration for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-\(0\) Albanese-witness argument: rigidity of morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, \textbf{over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero} (the alg-closed pivot of \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot" below; descent of the constancy conclusion to a general base field \(k\) is a separate downstream step, hosted in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). The classical statement (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4) is
18	
19	\begin{quote}
20	  \emph{Let \(A\) be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Then every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant.}
21	\end{quote}
22	
23	This is the keystone classical input for the C.2.d sub-step of Jacobian.tex \S C.2: the genus-\(0\) universal-property step of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} that concludes the universal-pointed-morphism factorisation by ruling out positive-dimensional images of \(f \colon C \to A\) when \(C\) has genus \(0\) and a \(k\)-rational marked point.
24	
25	\paragraph{Disposition (iter-155): \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} is a gated NAMED GAP.} The named declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} is landed with a \texttt{sorry} body and is held as a \textbf{named gap} in the same family as \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Jacobian.tex) and the deferred Serre-duality / Riemann--Roch stack --- honestly disclosed, not on a prover lane. The substantive content (the C.2.d sub-step below, ruling out a \(1\)-dimensional image of \(f\)) reduces to the vanishing of the cotangent pull-back \(\mathrm df \colon f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\), and a decisive iter-155 \texttt{mathlib-analogist} consult (\texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}) established that \textbf{producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is irreducibly a global-sections fact}, gated on two genuinely-absent inputs:
26	
27	\begin{itemize}
28	  \item \textbf{(gap i) Global cotangent-triviality of \(A\):} \(\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_A^{\oplus g}\), generated by translation-invariant differentials. The presheaf globalisation that would build this (\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) was \textbf{excised from the Lean tree at iter-145}; \texttt{GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity} \(+\) \texttt{shearMulRight} survive only as a \emph{design template}. Estimated \(\sim 800\)--\(1500\) LOC. Mathlib's only ``group cotangent is trivial'' idiom is the smooth-manifold \texttt{GroupLieAlgebra} stack, which does not port to schemes.
29	  \item \textbf{(gap ii) Serre duality \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)} for the genus-\(0\) curve. Given the global trivialisation of gap (i), \(\mathrm df \in H^0\bigl(C, \mathcal{H}om(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr) \cong \bigl(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k})\bigr)^{\oplus g}\), so \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\). Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf and no Riemann--Roch; the project's \texttt{Cohomology/*} stack computes \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (genus) but has \textbf{no \(H^0\)-vanishing lemma and no genus\(\leftrightarrow\Omega\) bridge}. The genus-\(0\) hypothesis \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) does \emph{not} yield \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) without the duality. Estimated \(\sim 3000\)--\(8000\) LOC.
30	\end{itemize}
31	
32	\noindent The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM), \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}, and the delegate \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}), which \emph{is} closed and axiom-clean, supplies only the \textbf{converse} half of the bridge: ``if \(\mathrm df = 0\) then \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) (is constant)''. It \emph{cannot} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\): on each affine chart \(V \subseteq C\), \(\Omega_{C/\bar k}(V)\) is free of rank \(1\) (nonzero), so a chart-by-chart computation never detects the vanishing --- the vanishing materialises only on global sections. Earlier framings in this chapter (the piece-(iv) ``DEFERRED, not on the C.2.d critical path'' disposition and the ``No-Serre-duality layer'') asserted that the chart-algebra route \emph{avoids} Serre duality; \textbf{that is false}, and is corrected throughout \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: \(H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) is \emph{on} the critical path for producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), not avoided.
33	
34	The project does \textbf{not} open a ``produce \(\mathrm df = 0\)'' prover lane until one of two candidate closure routes is built; the decision between them is a leading open strategic question and the chapter commits to neither:
35	\begin{itemize}
36	  \item \textbf{Route (a):} build gaps (i) \(+\) (ii) directly.
37	  \item \textbf{Route (b):} the dual-abelian-variety keystone --- ``morphisms \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) are constant because \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\)''. Earlier rejected as ``needs \(\Pic^0\)'', but since the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (Route A) needs the \(\Pic^0\) engine \emph{regardless}, route (b) may amortise the shared machinery and be globally cheaper than a standalone Serre-duality build.
38	\end{itemize}
39	
40	\paragraph{Iter-152 alg-closed pivot.} The project committed an architectural pivot: rigidity is proved \textbf{only over an algebraically closed base field \(\bar k\) of characteristic zero}, and the constancy conclusion descends to a general base field \(k\) downstream. The reason is that the chart-algebra core lemma \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (KDM) is mathematically \emph{false} as a bare \(B\)-only algebra lemma: a prover lane exhibited two counterexamples that satisfy every hypothesis yet have \(\ker D \supsetneq \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap})\) --- namely \(B = k \times k\) (finite \'etale over \(k\)) and \(B = \mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)\) over \(\mathbb Q\). The missing content is precisely ``\(k\) algebraically closed in \(B\)''. Adding the \emph{joint} hypotheses \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[IsDomain B]} makes it true: \texttt{[IsDomain B]} kills the \(B = k \times k\) counterexample and \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} kills the \(\mathbb Q(\sqrt 2)/\mathbb Q\) counterexample. (Neither hypothesis suffices alone; the iter-151 note that ``\texttt{[IsDomain B]} alone fails'' considered the hypotheses only one-at-a-time.) Over an algebraically closed base the \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} constants lemma also collapses to a one-liner via \texttt{IsAlgClosed.algebraMap\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}, and the entire (S3.\textsc{sep}/\textsc{pi}) base-change-to-\(\bar k\) chain is descoped from the M2.a critical path.
41	
42	\textbf{The Lean declaration's signature now carries \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}} (matching the theorem's existing docstring, which already described \(\bar k\) as algebraically closed --- the hypothesis was simply absent from the formal statement). Prior strategy iterations had instead routed through base-change to \(\bar k\) followed by Galois descent of morphism equality (M2.c, \(\sim 4\)--\(8\) iter / \(300\)--\(500\) LOC); the iter-127 framing then claimed all three pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile build over an \emph{arbitrary} base field, dropping descent entirely. The alg-closed pivot supersedes that framing for the rigidity theorem itself: rigidity is established over \(\bar k\), and the descent of the constancy conclusion to a general \(k\) is a short downstream consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (faithfully-flat covers are epimorphisms of schemes), with the detailed descent living in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter. The group-scheme cotangent-triviality pieces (i)/(iii) below remain genuinely intrinsic to the group scheme and are stated over a general base where that is sound; only the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (the KDM/constants core) requires the alg-closed base.

44	\section{Statement}
45	
46	% NOTE: classical rigidity for morphisms from \(\mathbb P^1\) (or genus-0
47	% pointed proper curves) to abelian / group varieties; standard
48	% references are Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch.~II \S4 (rational
49	% curves on abelian varieties; paywalled, not bundled) and Hartshorne,
50	% *Algebraic Geometry*, Ex.~IV.4 (not bundled in references/). The
51	% cotangent-route closure used here ties into Stacks Tag 04QM (smooth
52	% over fields ⇒ geometrically reduced; references/stacks-varieties.tex,
53	% lemma-smooth-geometrically-normal) for the chart-level differential
54	% machinery.
55	\begin{theorem}
56	  \label{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar}
58	  \leanok
59	  % SOURCE: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4 (verbatim text not yet
60	  % retrieved — paywalled, no open copy in references/). No SOURCE QUOTE
61	  % is supplied: the source is not bundled and recalled text must not be
62	  % substituted (citation discipline).
63	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, II §4.}
64	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero (Lean: \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} \(+\) \texttt{[CharZero kbar]}; in the use case, the algebraic closure of a base field \(k\), with descent of the conclusion to \(k\) handled downstream in the genus-\(0\) witness chapter). These two hypotheses match the theorem's existing docstring and are required by the curve-side \(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constancy chain (\cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}, \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}); see \S "Iter-152 alg-closed pivot". Let \(C : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\) (i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\)). Let \(A : \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\), with identity element \(\eta_A \in A(\bar k)\). Let
65	  \[
66	    f \colon C \longrightarrow A
67	  \]
68	  be a morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes over \(\Spec \bar k\), and let \(p \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point such that \(f(p) = \eta_A\). Then \(f\) equals the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\):
69	  \[
70	    f \;=\; \bigl(C \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A\bigr).
71	  \]
72	
73	  \emph{Encoding note (iter-126).} The Lean signature uses the abstract characterization \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{IsProper}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ C.\mathtt{hom} + \genus(C) = 0\) instead of a literal \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) scheme. The two encodings are equivalent over \(\bar k\) once the curve has a \(\bar k\)-rational point (Brauer--Severi triviality + Riemann--Roch on \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)); the abstract form is used because Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} does not ship a packaged \(\mathtt{ProjectiveSpace}\,n\,S\) as a \(\mathtt{Scheme.Over}\,S\), and the C.2.b–C.2.d proof decomposition below works for any smooth proper geometrically integral genus-0 curve over \(\bar k\) without explicit \(\mathbb P^1\) API.
74	\end{theorem}
75	
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}
78	  
79	  The conclusion is vacuously true when \(A\) is zero-dimensional: in that case \(A = \Spec \bar k\) and every morphism \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) trivially. The substantive content of the theorem is the positive-dimensional case, which decomposes into the steps of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}.
80	\end{remark}
81	
82	\section{Proof decomposition}
83	\label{sec:RigidityKbar_proof_decomposition}
84	
85	The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} decomposes as follows. We reuse the sub-step labelling of Jacobian.tex \S C.2 so cross-references between the two chapters remain unambiguous. \textbf{This decomposition is the eventual closing recipe for a gated named gap} (see the iter-155 disposition in the chapter introduction): the substantive step C.2.d reduces to producing \(\mathrm df = 0\), which is gated on gaps (i)\(+\)(ii) [route (a)] or on the dual-AV keystone [route (b)]. The steps below stay inert --- the Lean body is a \texttt{sorry} --- until a route is chosen and built; none names a present, assembled Mathlib infrastructure for the image-dimension and \(\mathrm df = 0\) content.
86	
87	\begin{itemize}
88	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let \(c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) denote the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\). The conclusion \(f = c\) follows from \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (i.e.\ \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, after the iter-125 refactor) applied to \(g_1 := f\), \(g_2 := c\), \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), \(Y := A\), once we produce a non-empty open \(U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) on which \(U.\iota \circ f.\mathrm{left} = U.\iota \circ c.\mathrm{left}\) as scheme morphisms. We will take \(U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). The refactored rigidity lemma's hypotheses are trivially satisfied: \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is smooth (hence reduced) and geometrically irreducible over \(\bar k\), and \(A\) is proper (hence separated) over \(\bar k\).
89	
90	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism \(f\) goes from a proper geometrically integral \(\bar k\)-scheme of dimension \(1\) to a smooth proper \(\bar k\)-scheme of dimension \(g := \dim_{\bar k} A\). The set-theoretic image \(f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A\) is an irreducible closed subset of \(A\) (continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map). \emph{Gating note (iter-155): this dichotomy names no present, assembled Mathlib infrastructure} --- the ``image of a proper map is closed and irreducible'' fact and a usable notion of scheme/image dimension are not packaged for this setting in snapshot \texttt{b80f227}; C.2.c is part of the gated assembly, to be detailed once a route (a)/(b) is chosen. Dichotomy:
91	  \begin{itemize}
92	    \item If \(g = 0\): \(A = \Spec \bar k\) and the conclusion is vacuous (\cref{rem:rigidity_over_kbar_dim_dichotomy}).
93	    \item If \(g \geq 1\) and the image is a single point: the image must be \(\{\eta_A\}\) (the pointed hypothesis \(f(p) = \eta_A\) pins it). Then \(f\) is constant on underlying topological points, and the conclusion follows by promoting set-level equality to scheme-morphism equality (sub-step C.2.e of Jacobian.tex, applied via \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}).
94	    \item If \(g \geq 1\) and the image is one-dimensional: the image is an irreducible rational curve in \(A\). This case is ruled out by the keystone classical input (C.2.d below).
95	  \end{itemize}
96	
97	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Keystone: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant --- the gated content.} The remaining case --- the image of \(f\) has dimension exactly \(1\) --- is ruled out by Mumford's classical fact stated in the chapter introduction, and is the load-bearing reduction of the whole theorem. It reduces to the vanishing \(\mathrm df = 0\) of the cotangent pull-back \(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{C/\bar k}\). Per the iter-155 disposition (chapter introduction) and \texttt{analogies/df-zero-production-iter155.md}, producing \(\mathrm df = 0\) is a \textbf{global-sections} fact: with the global trivialisation \(f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k} \cong \mathcal O_C^{\oplus g}\) (gap i), \(\mathrm df = 0 \iff H^0(C, \Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\) (gap ii, Serre duality on the genus-\(0\) curve). The two standard proofs sketched in Jacobian.tex \S C.2.d furnish the two gated closure routes that the project commits to neither of yet: \textbf{route (a)} = the cotangent-bundle route, building gaps (i)\(+\)(ii) directly (triviality of \(\Omega_{A/\bar k}\) + \(H^0(C,\Omega_{C/\bar k}) = 0\)); \textbf{route (b)} = the dual-abelian-variety route via \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\), which may amortise the \(\Pic^0\) engine that the positive-genus arm of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} needs regardless. The chart-algebra envelope of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} supplies only the converse ``\(\mathrm df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constant''; it does \emph{not} produce \(\mathrm df = 0\), and (contrary to earlier framing) does not avoid the Serre-duality input.
98	
99	  \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promotion of set-level equality to scheme-morphism equality.} Standard reduced-source / separated-target argument, integrated into the application of \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} in C.2.b.
100	\end{itemize}
